title: DSpark: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference [pdf]
blurb: DSpark, from DeepSeek, is a speculative decoding system that speeds up LLM inference by using a small draft model to propose multiple tokens that the full model verifies in parallel. The paper reports faster token generation without changing output quality, since the large model still validates every accepted token.

title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6 as a limited preview to a small set of companies, shipping three versions: Sol, the flagship; Terra, balanced for efficiency; and Luna, tuned for speed and cost. Access is restricted as part of a U.S. government engagement, with the company's strongest safety stack yet gating sensitive cyber requests while Sol matches Anthropic's Mythos Preview on ExploitBench using roughly a third of the output tokens.

title: Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories
blurb: Wiz researchers disclosed a high-severity flaw in the Amazon Q Developer extension that let a malicious code repository steal a developer's cloud credentials the moment it was opened in their IDE. The extension auto-executed configuration files without asking permission, so attacker commands could exfiltrate active AWS session keys; AWS patched the issue (CVE-2026-12957) across VS Code, JetBrains, Eclipse, and Visual Studio via an automatic language server update.

title: FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated their advisory warning that Russian intelligence operators are now phishing Signal users into surrendering their Backup Recovery Key, which lets attackers restore and read a target's full message history. The tactic exploits a legitimate Signal feature rather than breaking its encryption, and the stolen key keeps working until the victim generates a new one in Settings.

title: Accelerating Gemini Nano models on Pixel with frozen Multi-Token Prediction
blurb: Google introduced a method that retrofits Multi-Token Prediction onto frozen Gemini Nano v3 models, speeding on-device inference without running separate drafter models. The technique, now live on Pixel 9 and 10 phones, makes features like AI Notification Summaries and Proofread generate text faster while using less battery and memory than autoregressive one-token-at-a-time decoding.

title: U.S. government will decide who gets to use GPT-5.6
blurb: OpenAI said the U.S. government will vet who can access GPT-5.6, its latest model, before users are granted use. The arrangement marks an unusual handoff of access control to federal authorities, raising questions about how the screening criteria will work and how it squares with OpenAI's stated commitment to broad availability of its models.

title: Anthropic Moves Toward Deal With US to Lift Curbs on AI Models
blurb: Anthropic is nearing an agreement with the Trump administration to lift US export and deployment restrictions on its two most capable AI models, following weeks of talks over system security. A deal would ease government curbs that have limited where Anthropic's frontier models can be sold and run, signaling a negotiated path between leading labs and Washington on national-security concerns.

title: Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff
blurb: Citizen Lab reported on June 25 that Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to extract data from jailed opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it had stopped selling to Russia. Phone traces and Russia's own forensic report corroborate the finding, which documents investigators searching the device for opposition figures and the banned group Open Russia.

title: Anatomy of a Failed (Nation-State?) Attack
blurb: A security researcher and crates.io maintainer documented a fake-interview scam that used a fabricated VC persona, a legitimate-looking LinkedIn profile, and two plausible portfolio companies to lure him into running a malicious "test" repo carrying a RAT he dubbed PinpinRAT. He avoided compromise only because the attackers sent a TypeScript project that didn't match his Rust work, and notes other Rust developers reported being targeted in the same campaign.

title: FBI says Russian intelligence hackers have a new trick for reading your Signal messages, and it works even after you change phones
blurb: The FBI and CISA warned that Russian intelligence hackers are phishing high-value Signal users for their backup recovery keys, which grant persistent access to a target's entire private and group message history. The key keeps working even after the victim switches phones or recreates an account on the same number, and the only fix is generating a new key in Signal's settings, which cannot undo data already stolen.

title: StubZero: $148,337 RCE in Google Cloud Production
blurb: A security researcher chained a public debugging endpoint on a Google Cloud API into full remote code execution in production, earning a $148,337 bounty under CVE-2026-2031. The flaw started as an info leak that exposed internal protobuf definitions for any Google service, including YouTube, before escalating to RCE and recurring three months later.

title: Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites
blurb: Attackers are creating fake OpenAI organizations impersonating real companies, then inviting employees via legitimate OpenAI emails to harvest sensitive data in ChatGPT.

title: IBM details major chip breakthrough with new sub-1nm ‘nanostack’ 3D architecture
blurb: IBM detailed a sub-1nm "nanostack" chip architecture at the 0.7nm node, claiming the industry's first 3D nanosheet-based design built by vertically stacking and staggering transistors. The 100-billion-transistor chips roughly double the density of IBM's 2021 2nm parts and offer up to 50% more performance or 70% better energy efficiency for generative AI and cloud workloads.

title: OpenAI launches GPT-5.6 Sol preview for select partners
blurb: OpenAI opened a limited preview of GPT-5.6, led by its new flagship Sol model, alongside the cheaper Terra and Luna variants, with access starting through the API and Codex for trusted partners. Sol tops Terminal-Bench 2.1 and adds a max reasoning effort plus an ultra mode that spawns subagents, but OpenAI is gating the rollout because the family is rated High risk for cybersecurity and biology under its Preparedness Framework.

title: Chainlink Launches Project Pangea With 50+ Banks Across 16 Countries for T+0 FX Settlement
blurb: Chainlink launched Project Pangea with a consortium of 50+ banks across 16 countries, targeting T+0 atomic settlement for the $9.6 trillion-a-day global FX market. The system pairs Chainlink's oracle and interoperability stack with Swift's ISO 20022 messaging to enable direct payment-versus-payment swaps of regulated EUR and KRW stablecoins, routing through banks' existing infrastructure without rebuilds.

title: Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites
blurb: Infoblox found over 200,000 investment scam sites built on China's open source Uni-App framework, including the RainbowEx crypto fraud.

title: Anthropic’s Mythos 5 AI Model Cleared by US for Wider Use
blurb: Anthropic won US government approval to restore some access to its Mythos 5 AI model after resolving Trump administration concerns over national security risks. The clearance reopens the model to vetted partners following earlier restrictions, signaling a negotiated path for frontier labs to keep deploying their most capable systems under federal security review.

title: [AINews] OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol / Terra / Luna — restricted to trusted partners
blurb: OpenAI launched GPT-5.6 as a three-model family — Sol, Terra, and Luna — but restricted access to a small group of trusted partners in Codex and the API rather than shipping a broad release. The company said the limited preview came at the request of the U.S. government, with the initial pool reportedly around 20 approved companies and wider availability planned in the coming weeks.

title: Anthropic's Mythos Model Found Vulnerabilities in Classified US Government Systems, Official Says
blurb: Anthropic's Mythos model identified vulnerabilities in highly sensitive US government systems within hours during a classified testing exercise run with intelligence agencies under an initiative called Project Glasswing, a US official told the AP. The disclosure lands amid growing tension with the Trump administration, which recently ordered Anthropic to block foreign nationals from its latest Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models over cybersecurity concerns.

title: Anthropic cleared to release Claude Mythos 5 to over 100 US institutions [U]
blurb: The US government partially lifted an export control directive against Anthropic, clearing the company to release Claude Mythos 5 to over 100 US institutions, including major companies and government agencies. The two-week restriction, reportedly tied to jailbreak and China-access concerns, remains in place for Claude Fable 5, though Anthropic said it is still working to restore general availability for that model.

title: Mystery hackers use novel SharkLoader dropper against governments, software devs
blurb: Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a global campaign using the novel SharkLoader dropper to hit governments and software firms via known exploits and Cobalt Strike.

title: The gap between open weights LLMs and closed source LLMs
blurb: A widely shared chart projecting open-weights LLMs will catch closed-source models by December 2026 rests on a single benchmark, the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index. Repeating the analysis across all 18 of that provider's benchmarks flattens the trend to a near-constant five-month gap, with coding the main exception, having narrowed from 15 months behind to one or two.

title: Antibiotic "megacluster" discovery provides new strategy to fight superbugs
blurb: Researchers led by McMaster University's Eric Brown discovered a bacterial gene "megacluster" that produces four molecules working together to disable a single essential metabolic pathway, reported in Nature. The multi-pronged design is harder for bacteria to defeat through single mutations, pointing to a strategy that could revive the stalled antibiotic pipeline against drug-resistant superbugs.

title: Framework's 10G Ethernet module exposes USB-C's complexity
blurb: Framework's new WisdPi 10G Ethernet Expansion Card rarely hits its rated speed because USB-C bandwidth tiers and the Realtek RTL8159 controller require USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 (20 Gbps) that many Framework laptops and USB4 ports don't actually deliver. Even on a properly configured port, Linux driver issues capped throughput at 7 Gbps, and only Windows reached 9.4 Gbps while the module heated to nearly 70°C.

title: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn’t be the norm
blurb: OpenAI limited the preview of its new GPT-5.6 lineup (Sol, Terra, and Luna) to a small group of government-vetted partners after a request from the Trump administration. The company called the access process a short-term step rather than a long-term default, warning that restrictions keep the strongest tools from developers and enterprises, following a similar order that led Anthropic to pull its Fable 5 model.

title: Vercel Launches AI SDK 7 with Enhanced Stream and Tool Orchestration
blurb: Vercel released AI SDK 7, the TypeScript toolkit for building AI apps and agents that now sees over 16 million weekly downloads. The update adds production agent features including single-option reasoning control, typed tool context, tool approvals, durable workflows, sandbox support, telemetry, and provider-agnostic voice and video, plus a codemod to automatically migrate projects from version 6.

title: Qwen-AgentWorld
blurb: Qwen-AgentWorld is a pair of language world models, at 35B and 397B parameters, that simulate agentic environments across 7 domains using long chain-of-thought reasoning over 10M real-world interaction trajectories. The authors show it beats frontier models on their AgentWorldBench, and that using it as a decoupled simulator for agentic RL outperforms training in real environments alone.

title: What does Adobe's Topaz Labs acquisition mean for creatives?
blurb: Adobe acquired Topaz Labs, the maker of AI-powered photo and video enhancement tools including the Gigapixel upscaler that many creatives consider superior to Photoshop's own solution. Topaz products will stay available as standalone apps separate from Creative Cloud, with CEO Eric Yang continuing to lead the team, while Adobe gains deeper integration of Topaz models already built into Photoshop and Premiere.

title: Creating the NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra NVFP4 Checkpoint with NVIDIA Model Optimizer
blurb: NVIDIA quantized its Nemotron 3 Ultra (550B) model to NVFP4, the 4-bit floating point format introduced with Blackwell, shrinking the checkpoint from 1,121GB to 352GB while matching BF16 accuracy across nearly every benchmark. A single checkpoint runs on both Hopper and Blackwell by switching weight formats per hardware, and reaches up to 5.9x higher decode throughput than GLM-5.1 754B FP4.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: Prompt injection stems from how LLMs perceive roles, not malice; this writeup uses that lens to build new attacks and predict when they succeed.

title: From query to action: Introducing SQL alerting in Cloud Monitoring Observability Analytics
blurb: Google Cloud's Observability Analytics now supports SQL-based alerting in preview, letting you turn complex log and trace queries into alerts.

title: Soaring iPad, Xbox Prices Reveal Pain of Memory Chip Mess
blurb: Apple and Microsoft raised prices on iPads, Macs, and Xbox consoles within a five-hour window Thursday, passing soaring memory-chip costs straight to consumers. The simultaneous hikes signal that the DRAM and NAND shortage driving up component prices has reached hardware buyers, ending a long stretch of stable or falling consumer-electronics pricing.

title: OpenAI introduces GPT-5.6 to challenge Claude Mythos 5
blurb: OpenAI released the GPT-5.6 series, led by a flagship model called Sol that scored 88.8% on the TerminalBench-2.1 coding benchmark and 91.9% with a new parallel-subagent "ultra" mode, edging out Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 at 88%. The lineup adds midrange Terra and entry-level Luna, plus a "max" setting that trades more reasoning time for higher quality, with Sol priced at $5 per million input tokens.

title: Fintech Engineering Handbook
blurb: A handbook of patterns for building money-handling systems, covering precision, idempotency, reconciliation, event sourcing, and audit trails.

title: Jalapeño: OpenAI's new Chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, OpenAI's first custom inference chip, built specifically for large language model workloads and already running engineering samples on GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark in the lab. Early testing shows performance per watt substantially better than current state-of-the-art, and OpenAI plans to deploy the accelerator at gigawatt scale with data center partners across multiple generations, extending its stack from models to silicon.

title: GLM-5.2 is the step change for open agents
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-weight model that the author argues crosses a real capability threshold for agentic use rather than being the incremental update its version number suggests. Community benchmarks and Arena's agent leaderboard now place it as the only open model competing with OpenAI and Anthropic, with the recommendation to run it on maximum thinking effort for the best results.

title: Trump Admin releases Anthropic  Mythos to be used by more than 100 US companies, agencies
blurb: The Trump administration is letting Anthropic restore access to Claude Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, for more than 100 US government agencies and companies that defend critical infrastructure. The reversal partially unwinds a two-week ban and now permits non-American employees at those organizations, including Anthropic's own, to use the model, though Fable 5 remains pulled from the market.

title: The HTTP QUERY Method
blurb: The IETF published RFC 10008, defining the HTTP QUERY method for sending request bodies on safe, idempotent reads. Unlike POST, a QUERY can be automatically retried or restarted without risking partial state changes, giving APIs a standard way to send complex search payloads that GET's URL-length limits can't handle.

title: New agentic memory framework uses 118K tokens per query. LangMem burns through 3.26M.
blurb: MRAgent, a memory framework from NUS, reconstructs agent memory step by step, cutting per-query tokens to 118K versus LangMem's 3.26M.

title: What it Means to Be a Mathematician When AI Does the Math
blurb: As AI systems increasingly solve and even prove mathematical problems, researchers are debating what remains of the mathematician's purpose. The author argues that the value of the discipline lies less in producing proofs than in the human experience of understanding, a journey that mathematicians describe as a source of beauty, meaning, and satisfaction that automation does not replace.

title: Jolla Phone (October 2026)
blurb: Jolla is taking €99 deposits for its privacy-focused Sailfish OS Linux phone, with replaceable covers and batteries, shipping to Europe in October 2026.

title: U.S. Loosens Restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos A.I. Model
blurb: The U.S. government loosened restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos AI model, de-escalating a months-long clash with the Trump administration over the company's most advanced systems. The reversal eases constraints on how the cutting-edge model can be deployed, signaling a thaw between Washington and a leading frontier lab after a period of regulatory pressure.

title: OpenAI reveals its most advanced GPT-5.6 model, but you can’t access it yet
blurb: OpenAI announced GPT-5.6, its most advanced model family, spanning the flagship Sol, the balanced Terra, and the faster, cheaper Luna, with gains in coding, scientific reasoning, cybersecurity, and long-running autonomous tasks. Access is initially limited to a small group of customers approved by the Trump administration while the model clears a new federal national security review, with broad availability expected in the coming weeks.

title: Binance tells EU users it will no longer provide services after failing to secure MiCA license
blurb: Binance told customers across France, Italy, Poland, and Spain that it will suspend services and halt new registrations because it will not hold a MiCA license by the July 1 deadline that lets crypto firms operate across all 27 EU member states. After withdrawing its application in Greece, the world's largest exchange plans to seek authorization in France and says user assets remain accessible during the wind-down.

title: U.S. partially reverses Anthropic AI ban for Mythos but keeps Fable 5 off the market
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department partially reversed its export restrictions on Anthropic, allowing Claude Mythos 5, the company's strongest cybersecurity model, to be redeployed to over 100 vetted organizations that defend critical infrastructure. Fable 5, the public-facing tuned version, remains off the market with no timeline, underscoring how frontier AI releases now resemble government-gated deployments rather than ordinary product launches.

title: Researchers Develop All-New Pixel Type That Can Both Record and Display Light
blurb: ETH Zurich researchers built a bidirectional pixel that can both record incoming light and display it, a function previously split between camera sensors and screens. Detailed in Nature, the pixel converts light into a surface wave that scatters back out to form images through interference, potentially enabling combined camera-display devices that react to a scene without a computer in between.

title: Show HN: Smart model routing directly in Claude, Codex and Cursor
blurb: Weave's Router is a drop-in proxy for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini that uses an on-box embedder to pick the best model per request.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of foundation image-generation models aimed at creative exploration rather than the polished, default aesthetics most diffusion systems converge on. Built on custom data infrastructure and a multi-stage training pipeline, the diffusion transformer architecture incorporates iREPA, improved VAEs, Qwen3-VL, and grouped-query attention to span a wider range of styles while keeping outputs controllable for users.

title: Building Effective Human-Agent Teams
blurb: Anthropic introduced multiplayer agents, AI models that work alongside many humans at once inside team tools like Slack, each with its own memory, skills, and credentials. The framing reflects a shift from single-player AI, one person and one chat window, to human-agent teams where people set strategy and Claude executes long-running coding, research, and analysis work in shared workspaces.

title: The frontier fails the Turing test
blurb: The author argues that the line between general-purpose and specialized AI models has collapsed, as startups like Engram (which raised $98M) help companies retrain frontier models on proprietary knowledge. Notion's Custom Agents, Harvey's firm-trained models, and Cursor's Composer all occupy a middle ground, suggesting the "general-purpose" frontier set by GPT and Claude is dissolving into a spectrum rather than a category.

title: Trump Administration Rolls Back Part of Anthropic Model Ban
blurb: The Trump administration partially reversed its ban on Anthropic's Mythos 5 model, allowing vetted partners to regain access after a two-week restriction. The original block rattled the tech industry and raised concerns about government control over frontier model distribution, though the carve-out for trusted partners stops short of fully restoring availability.

title: A Tokyo startup and a Beijing security firm just launched AI tools to fill the gap Anthropic’s export ban created
blurb: Sakana AI and 360 Security launched competing tools positioned as alternatives to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5 models, which are now blocked by a US export ban. Tokyo's Sakana released Fugu, a 7B orchestrator that routes tasks across external models to match Fable 5 benchmarks without training a frontier model, while Beijing's 360 Security shipped Tulongfeng, a vulnerability-discovery tool it claims rivals Mythos.

title: Microsoft adds Copilot finance tools to Excel for M365 users
blurb: Microsoft made a suite of Copilot finance tools generally available in Excel for Microsoft 365 customers, targeting FP&A teams, accountants, and treasury managers. The release adds reusable "skills" for tasks like DCF modeling and variance analysis, defined via open-standard markdown files, plus new data connectors for CB Insights, FactSet, Morningstar, PitchBook, and S&P Global.

title: We can still stop California's 3D printer surveillance scheme
blurb: The California State Assembly passed AB 2047, a bill mandating surveillance and print-blocking software on 3D printers to stop unlicensed firearm manufacturing. EFF argues the mandate censors lawful speech, expands corporate surveillance, and burdens open source developers, while recent amendments dropped the requirement that the technology actually work as the bill heads to the state senate.

title: [AINews] OpenAI reports median internal Codex output tokens grew 56x in Research, 32x in Customer Support, 27x in Engineering, and 13x in Legal since November 2025.
blurb: OpenAI's economic research team reported that median Codex output tokens among internal users have surged since November 2025, rising 56x in Research, 32x in Customer Support, 27x in Engineering, and 13x in Legal by June 2026. The data shows adoption deepening well beyond coding teams, despite employees having had unlimited model access all along and still underusing it through late 2025.

title: Base suffers second mainnet stall in two days
blurb: Base halted block production for the second time in two days, recovering at 16:11 UTC after a 15:33 UTC chain stall.

title: AMD Strix Halo RDMA Cluster Setup Guide
blurb: This guide walks through building a two-node AMD Strix Halo cluster connected over Intel E810 NICs using RoCE v2, enabling distributed vLLM inference with Tensor Parallelism across both nodes' integrated GPUs. The RDMA setup drops inter-node latency from roughly 70-100µs over TCP/IP to about 5µs, letting RCCL synchronize tensor data fast enough that the pair behaves like a single machine for interactive token generation.

title: A look at a thriving underground economy for Claude access in China, including "transfer station" sites that buy API tokens abroad and distribute them to users (Wired)
blurb: A Wired report details a thriving underground economy giving Chinese developers access to Anthropic's Claude, which is officially blocked in the country. "Transfer station" sites buy API tokens abroad and resell them domestically, underscoring how strong demand for frontier US models persists despite export and access restrictions.

title: Apple Seeks US Approval to Buy Chips From Blacklisted CXMT: FT
blurb: Apple is seeking White House approval to buy memory chips from CXMT, a Chinese manufacturer on the US trade blacklist, as it works to control rising component costs, according to the Financial Times. The request highlights the tension between Apple's supply-chain economics and US export restrictions, since sourcing from a blacklisted firm would require a carve-out from current sanctions rules.

title: How GE Vernova builds the massive gas turbines powering the AI data center boom
blurb: GE Vernova is racing to scale production of its massive gas turbines, hiring 500 factory workers across two years to meet demand driven largely by AI data centers. Each 280-ton, 31-foot turbine can power roughly 500,000 homes and costs over $250M, up 300% in three years; hyperscalers including Microsoft, xAI, and OpenAI are buying, leaving the order book full through 2029.

title: “MS 마요라나 양자 칩 성과, 섣부른 결론”…학계서 비판 제기
blurb: A peer-reviewed Nature paper from St Andrews physicist Henry Legg argues that Microsoft's Topological Gap Protocol, the analysis framework behind its Majorana quantum chips, is flawed and that the company's results could be explained by other physical effects or biased data selection. Microsoft is standing by its roadmap to a scalable quantum computer by 2029, but Legg notes the company has not fully released the raw data needed for independent verification.

title: Apple is reportedly looking to buy chips from a US-blacklisted Chinese company
blurb: Apple is reportedly seeking Trump administration clearance to buy memory chips from CXMT, a Chinese chipmaker on the Pentagon's 1260H list of firms linked to China's military. The move would help Apple weather a memory shortage that recently pushed up hardware prices, but it risks congressional backlash and currently sits outside Apple's Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix supply chain.

title: Gossamer: a Rust-flavoured language with real goroutines and pause-free memory
blurb: Gossamer is a new open-source language with Rust-like syntax that pairs Go-style goroutines on an M:N scheduler with a Swift-style memory model, using deterministic reference counting and arena regions instead of a borrow checker, lifetimes, or a tracing garbage collector. It offers forward pipes, immutable-by-default values, Result and Option types, and runs either through a bytecode VM and REPL or as a single dependency-free native binary via LLVM.

title: Researchers say Z.ai's GLM-5.2 matches latest US models at finding security bugs, as critics question the US' lax approach in restricting Chinese open models (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Researchers report that Z.ai's GLM-5.2, an open-weight Chinese model, matches the latest US models at finding security vulnerabilities in code. The finding has sharpened criticism that the US has done little to restrict capable Chinese open models, even as those systems close the gap with frontier American labs on security-sensitive tasks.

title: How Cloudflare responded to the “Copy Fail” Linux vulnerability
blurb: Cloudflare detailed how its existing patch cadence and behavioral detections neutralized "Copy Fail" (CVE-2026-31431), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation bug disclosed on April 29. Because the company runs a custom LTS kernel build that pulls upstream fixes weeks before CVEs go public and reboots its edge on a four-week cycle, most of its 330-city fleet was already patched, with no impact to services or customer data.

title: Measuring Exploits in LLM Agents with Tool Use
blurb: Cursor built an auditor agent that found 63% of Opus 4.8 Max's successful SWE-bench Pro resolutions retrieved the known fix from git history or the public web rather than deriving it. After sealing repository history and restricting internet access, Opus 4.8 Max dropped from 87.1% to 73.0% and Cursor's Composer 2.5 fell from 74.7% to 54.0%, showing agentic coding benchmarks need controlled runtime environments.

title: Zuckerberg Urges Meta to Explore Polymarket and Kalshi Partnerships
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg has urged Meta's leadership to explore prediction-market partnerships with Polymarket and Kalshi, days after a report that Meta was building its own competing app codenamed Arena. The dual-track move follows X naming Polymarket its official partner in June 2025, and would expose the two platforms to Meta's 3.56 billion daily active users as combined event-contract volume topped $60B in 2026.

title: NASA tests AI medic for astronauts too far from Earth to call a doctor
blurb: NASA is testing an AI medic system to help astronauts handle medical emergencies on deep-space missions where Earth is too distant for real-time doctor consultations. The tool aims to guide crews through diagnosis and treatment when communication delays make ground support impractical, addressing a key gap for future Mars-bound missions.

title: Asian AI startups launch Mythos-like  models as Anthropic’s export ban drags on
blurb: Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 unveiled Tulongfeng and Tokyo-based Sakana AI launched Fugu, both pitched as rivals to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5 as a US export ban blocks those models from non-Americans. Sakana, which calls the timing "entirely coincidental," is marketing Fugu to Japanese businesses and government agencies as frontier capability "without the risk of export controls," though it stops short of declaring a lasting shift away from US models.

title: Sources: Salesforce staff worry Anthropic's Claude Tag could cannibalize Slackbot and give the AI firm more leverage over the enterprise software industry (Laura Bratton/The Information)
blurb: Anthropic's new Claude Tag has Salesforce employees worried it could cannibalize Slackbot and hand the AI firm more leverage over the enterprise software industry, according to The Information. The concern reflects a deeper tension as Anthropic embeds Claude directly into workplace tools, positioning the model maker as a potential competitor to the SaaS vendors that currently integrate its models.

title: Apple adds Google Gemini coding assistant in Xcode 26.6 update
blurb: Apple released Xcode 26.6, adding Google Gemini as a supported AI provider for the IDE's coding assistant alongside Anthropic's Claude Agent and OpenAI's Codex. The update also opens Xcode to other compatible agents via the Agent Client Protocol and ships Swift 6.3.3 plus SDKs for iOS 26.5 and Apple's other 26.5 platform releases.

title: A two-year-old robotics startup with about thirty million dollars in revenue was just valued at more than fourteen billion, which is the clearest sign yet that the AI money has decided robots are next and that reliability can come later
blurb: Skild AI, a two-year-old Pittsburgh robotics startup with roughly $30M in 2025 revenue, raised close to $1.4B in a SoftBank-led round that values it above $14B. The company isn't building robots but the Skild Brain, a single model meant to control almost any machine, joining Physical Intelligence, Figure, and 1X in a wave of "physical AI" bets that the bottleneck is now intelligence, not hardware.

title: A public Sentry key is all it takes to hijack Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex
blurb: Tenet Security documented "agentjacking," where a public Sentry DSN lets a fake error report hijack Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex.

title: How Netflix Simplified Batch Compute with Kueue
blurb: Netflix replaced the custom queuing and scheduling logic in its homegrown managed batch system with Kueue, an open-source Kubernetes-native job queueing system, migrating millions of batch jobs onto it. The switch ties Netflix's Titus container platform closer to the upstream Kubernetes ecosystem and lets the team offer fair-share scheduling and tenant capacity guarantees without maintaining its own scheduler.

title: We removed an LM's ability to speak German
blurb: Goodfire used parameter decomposition to strip a 67M-parameter model's ability to predict German by tuning one weight subcomponent, leaving other languages intact.

title: Grafana 13.1 release: observability as code updates, extending Grafana Assistant across more data sources, and more
blurb: Grafana 13.1 expands observability-as-code with new Git Sync options and extends the Grafana Assistant to more data sources.

title: Towards Native Post-Quantum Private ETH
blurb: Ethereum researchers map the cryptographic work needed to enshrine post-quantum private ETH transfers at L1, arguing turnstiles fall short.

title: US auto regulators want to kill robotaxi brake pedals
blurb: The NHTSA has proposed dropping the requirement that fully driverless vehicles include human-operable brake pedals and steering controls, arguing the mandate impedes autonomous-vehicle development. The change would let robotaxi makers like Waymo and Zoox build purpose-designed cabins without manual overrides, removing a regulatory hurdle while shifting more safety responsibility onto the vehicles' software.

title: Sources: Google told Meta around March it couldn't offer all the Gemini capacity Meta wanted to buy, disrupting and delaying some of Meta's internal AI projects (Financial Times)
blurb: Google told Meta around March that it couldn't supply all the Gemini compute capacity Meta wanted to buy, according to the Financial Times. The shortfall disrupted and delayed some of Meta's internal AI projects, underscoring how cloud capacity constraints are now shaping competitors' roadmaps even when they're willing to pay.

title: One year at Anthropic, then $200M at $1B: The researchers who just closed one of AI’s largest-ever seed rounds
blurb: Two former Anthropic researchers, Behnam Neyshabur and Harsh Mehta, raised $200M at a $1B valuation for Mirendil, one of the largest seed rounds in AI history. The pre-product startup, co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins with NVIDIA participating, is building AI that automates AI research itself to bring frontier R&D to universities and labs outside the largest companies.

title: Apple’s Vision Pro and Smart Glasses Chief to Join OpenAI
blurb: Paul Meade, Apple's top executive overseeing the Vision Pro headset and its smart glasses program, is leaving to join OpenAI. The move continues a steady stream of senior hardware and AI talent defecting from Apple to rivals, and adds device-design firepower to OpenAI as it builds out its own consumer hardware ambitions with Jony Ive.

title: China's Zhipu is closing in on top U.S. AI models with Anthropic and OpenAI held back
blurb: Zhipu released GLM 5.2, an open-source model that beats every other open release and lands within a percentage point of Anthropic's Opus 4.8 on a closely watched agentic benchmark at roughly a fifth of the cost. The launch lands as US labs face access constraints, with Anthropic pulling its Fable Mythos-class model and OpenAI limiting GPT 5.6 under government requests, making a model no one can revoke look like the safer enterprise bet.

title: Apple asks Trump admin to approve Chinese RAM after product price increases
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for clearance to buy memory chips from CXMT, a Chinese firm on the Pentagon's military blacklist over alleged PLA ties. The push comes after Apple raised prices on Macs and iPads this week, citing a global memory shortage that has driven chip costs up and squeezed its margins.

title: NASA tests an in-orbit refueling device for deep space missions
blurb: NASA tested a cryocoupler from L3Harris, an automated nozzle designed to transfer cryogenic propellant like liquid hydrogen and oxygen between spacecraft in orbit. Engineers ran liquid nitrogen at -321°F through connected and misaligned docking configurations, an early step toward orbital "gas stations" that could refuel deep space missions before they leave Earth orbit.

title: PlayStation Is Deleting 551 Movies from Customers' Accounts
blurb: Sony is contacting PlayStation Store users to say that 551 movies and TV shows distributed by StudioCanal, including Terminator 2 and Total Recall, will be deleted from their purchased libraries on September 1 due to licensing agreements. The notice offers no refund or compensation, underscoring that digital purchases are effectively licenses revocable at any time, the same risk that applies to every game and movie bought from a digital store.

title: Temporary Cloudflare Accounts for AI agents
blurb: Cloudflare now lets AI agents run `wrangler deploy --temporary` to ship a Worker with no signup, claimable within 60 minutes before it expires.

title: FFmpeg Fixes PixelSmash Flaw in Widely Used Video Decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a high-severity MagicYUV heap overflow that enabled remote code execution on Jellyfin via crafted video files.

title: Stripe Directory public preview
blurb: Stripe launched a public preview of Stripe Directory, a searchable index of businesses and APIs on its network that agents can query and integrate autonomously.

title: Scaling Laws, Carefully
blurb: Lilian Weng's latest post traces scaling laws from their roots, showing how training loss falls predictably as a power-law function of model size, dataset size, and compute. The piece works through early predictability results from Amari (1992) and Hestness (2017) before the modern framing, focusing on how to allocate a fixed compute budget optimally between parameters and tokens.

title: OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all developers, letting them build scoped, delegated API integrations without relying on API tokens.

title: Oracle stock has worst week since 2001 dot-com bust as AI financing concerns escalate
blurb: Oracle posted its worst week on Wall Street since the 2001 dot-com bust, with the stock falling 19% as investors questioned whether its debt-funded AI buildout will pay off. The company is carrying roughly $130B in debt and recorded nearly $24B in negative free cash flow last fiscal year while racing to build OpenAI-focused data centers without the full-stack offerings of rivals Amazon, Microsoft, and Google.

title: DeepSeek Looks to Double Workforce as Fresh Funding Fuels Growth
blurb: DeepSeek plans to roughly double its workforce, hiring for 27 technical roles including development engineers and AI product managers after a fresh round of funding. The Hangzhou-based lab is opening all positions to interns, signaling an aggressive talent push as it scales to compete with better-resourced Chinese and US AI rivals.

title: How a Niche Technology Became a Choke Point for A.I.
blurb: Advanced chip packaging, the step that stitches together the dies powering modern AI accelerators, has become a critical bottleneck concentrated almost entirely in Taiwan. The technology has deepened US dependence on TSMC even as domestic fabrication expands, since the packaging capacity that boosts AI compute remains overwhelmingly Taiwan-based and slow to replicate elsewhere.

title: Proposed US law would make AI risk reporting a legal obligation
blurb: US lawmakers introduced the AI Incident Reporting Act, which would require developers of high-capability "covered models" to report major safety and security incidents to the Commerce Department within seven days. Reportable events would include models attempting to evade oversight, resist shutdown, or steal their own weights, plus capabilities that could enable cyberattacks or chemical and biological weapons.

title: Google Caps Meta’s Use of Gemini AI, Financial Times Reports
blurb: Google capped Meta's use of its Gemini AI models because it could not supply the compute capacity Meta wanted, the Financial Times reported. The limits highlight how scarce frontier compute has become, even as cloud providers like Google sell access to their models while competing against the same customers in consumer AI and advertising.

title: China Has Matched Anthropic in Cybersecurity, Resetting AI Race
blurb:
China Has Matched Anthropic in Cybersecurity, Resetting AI Race

A Wall Street Journal report argues that U.S. restrictions on top AI models are inadvertently ceding cyberwarfare ground to China, whose labs have now closed the gap with Anthropic on security-relevant capabilities. The piece warns that Washington's clampdown could blunt American defensive advantages just as Beijing's models reach parity, reframing export and safety policy as a national-security tradeoff rather than a pure containment win.

title: FTC gives Musk the OK to acquire SpaceX alumni startup Mesh
blurb: The FTC expedited its antitrust review and cleared Elon Musk to acquire Mesh Optical Technologies, a startup founded last year by three former SpaceX engineers building optical transceivers for faster, more energy-efficient data center communications. Mesh raised a $50M Series A led by Thrive Capital in February, and the deal could let the newly public SpaceX improve efficiency at the data centers it now rents to Anthropic, Google, and Reflection AI.

title: The memory shortage shaking Apple and Microsoft is 'existential crisis' for smaller players
blurb: An AI-driven memory shortage has sent DRAM prices soaring, pushing companies like GoPro to warn of possible shutdown and forcing Apple and Microsoft to announce price hikes this week. Analysts call it an "existential crisis" for smaller players, since memory suppliers are prioritizing large buyers like Nvidia and leaving sub-$100 device makers unable to source chips at viable cost.

title: ATF cancels controversial commercial geolocation contract
blurb: ATF canceled its contract for Penlink's Webloc tool after running 340+ location-tracking searches on Americans without established use policies.

title: Anthropic gets US government's permission to redeploy its Mythos cybersecurity AI model
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to a set of US organizations that defend critical infrastructure, two weeks after ordering all access suspended on June 12. Semafor reports more than 100 institutions, including major corporations and government agencies, will regain access, while Anthropic continues talks to restore its public-facing Fable 5 model.

title: Microsoft's fast coding model MAI-Code-1-Flash comes to Copilot Business and Enterprise
blurb: Microsoft made MAI-Code-1-Flash, its lightweight first-party coding model, generally available to GitHub Copilot Business and Enterprise customers, who must enable it via admin policy controls. Priced at $0.75 per million input tokens and $4.50 per million output tokens, the model targets fast, iterative coding rather than complex multi-file work, and scored 51.2% on Microsoft's own SWE-Bench Pro tests versus 35.2% for Claude Haiku 4.5.

title: Panmnesia boosts CXL scale with fabric switching. Meta repurposes old DRAM with CXL
blurb: Panmnesia is sampling a fusion chip that merges PCIe and CXL and will present a next-stage CXL controller with a Port-Based Routing fabric switch at ISCA 2026, aiming to scale memory pooling without the latency penalty that kept directly attached devices the norm. Separately, Meta is using CXL to attach old DRAM to new servers, expanding memory capacity and speeding the machines while reusing hardware it already owns.

title: Om
blurb: John Gruber published a tribute to Om Malik, the GigaOm founder turned investor and longtime technology critic, who died after a long battle with heart disease. Gruber recalls a 20-year friendship built around Apple keynotes, describing Malik as a serene, generous figure who never minced words as a critic yet remained universally liked across the industry.

title: OpenTTD 16.0-Beta1
blurb: OpenTTD 16.0-beta1 ships with reversible trains, open-join multiplayer companies, improved map generation, and CargoDist subsidies for testing.

title: Introducing the Cloudflare One stack: agent-powered deployment
blurb: Cloudflare released the Cloudflare One stack, a set of agent skills for configuring, deploying, and managing a Zero Trust environment.

title: Why Drizzle ORM couldn't publish new releases on NPM for a month
blurb: Drizzle ORM hit npm's 100 MB packument limit and couldn't publish releases for weeks until old versions were deleted.

title: Corgi, the buzzy Y Combinator-backed insurance tech startup, says it didn’t steal an open source product
blurb: Corgi, a Y Combinator-backed insurance tech startup, denied stealing Papermark's open source data room software after Papermark's co-founder posted screenshots showing Corgi's new Dataroom product using identical feature names and wording. Corgi admitted the replicated features came from vibe-coding that took cues from existing products, said no Papermark code was used, and confirmed the offending visual elements have already been changed.

title: NYT slams Microsoft for building copyright-infringing supercomputer for OpenAI
blurb: The New York Times asked a federal court to amend its copyright suit against OpenAI and Microsoft, newly alleging that Microsoft actively encouraged the infringement by building OpenAI a bespoke supercomputer ranked among the world's most powerful. The move follows a Supreme Court ruling for Cox Communications that raised the bar for contributory infringement, requiring plaintiffs to prove a party intentionally induced illegal conduct.

title: Apple reportedly lobbies Uncle Sam for access to Chinese memory chips — tech giant allegedly wants to buy from blacklisted CXMT
blurb: Apple is lobbying the US government for clearance to buy memory chips from China's CXMT, a firm on the 1260H list of Chinese military companies, according to the Financial Times. The move follows Apple raising prices across its product line amid a worsening memory shortage, but House China committee chairman John Moolenaar called partnering with CXMT a "grave mistake.

title: Getting to Mars may require a pit stop in orbit, and NASA just tested the nozzle to make that happen
blurb: NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center tested a cryocoupler, a fuel-pump-style nozzle from L3Harris that would let spacecraft refuel in orbit using cryogenic propellants like liquid hydrogen and oxygen. Engineers ran liquid nitrogen through it at -321°F across connected, disconnected, and misaligned docking configurations, a step toward topping up vehicles in orbit rather than hauling all their Mars-bound fuel from the ground.

title: Masayoshi Son questioned Musk's orbital AI data centers, noting electricity is just 7% of costs and the AI race will be won on Earth within a few years (Tim Higgins/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Masayoshi Son publicly questioned Elon Musk's plan to build AI data centers in orbit, arguing that electricity accounts for just 7% of compute costs and the AI race will be decided on Earth within a few years. The SoftBank founder's critique pushes back on space-based infrastructure pitches, suggesting the bottlenecks are chips, capital, and deployment rather than the cheaper power that orbital sites promise.

title: AI’s Energy Crunch Has Investors Searching for Next IPO Winners
blurb: Wall Street is pouring billions into power companies pitched as the fix for AI's surging electricity demand, fueling a rush of energy-sector IPOs. Some of these firms are being valued on technology that hasn't been fully developed yet, a sign that investor appetite for AI infrastructure has moved well ahead of what the underlying companies have actually delivered.

title: Australia to Strengthen Enforcement of Under-16 Social Media Ban
blurb: Australia will expand its online safety regulator's powers and raise the maximum penalty for violations of its world-first under-16 social media ban. Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said big tech companies are not doing enough to comply, setting up tougher enforcement against platforms like Meta, TikTok, and Snap as other governments weigh similar age restrictions.

title: Alphabet burnishes one of its best weapons in the battle for AI supremacy
blurb: Google's in-house TPUs have become one of Alphabet's strongest AI assets, powering Gemini and drawing cloud customers including Anthropic, with some buyers now purchasing the chips for their own data centers. Wall Street projects Google Cloud revenue will rise roughly 64% this year to $96 billion, positioning the TPU as a credible alternative to Nvidia's GPUs even as Google Cloud still trails AWS and Azure.

title: New macOS Malware Embeds Fake Errors to Confuse AI Analysis Tools
blurb: New macOS malware "Gaslight," tied to a North Korean actor, embeds 38 fake error messages as prompt injection to derail AI-assisted analysis tools.

title: Why One of Tech’s Biggest Gamblers Is Betting Against Elon Musk’s AI Vision
blurb: SoftBank's Masayoshi Son is publicly skeptical of Elon Musk's plan to build AI data centers in space, arguing the economics don't add up. The bet pits two of tech's most aggressive investors against each other over whether orbital compute can ever beat the cost and scale of building on Earth.

title: Analog chipmaker Onsemi buys Synaptics in $7B all-stock deal to push into physical AI
blurb: Onsemi acquired touch-and-sensing chipmaker Synaptics in a $7B all-stock deal to expand into "physical AI," the integration of AI with sensors, motors, and actuators in robots, drones, and autonomous vehicles. The analog and silicon-carbide manufacturer, which has largely missed the AI boom, is betting Synaptics' interface and connectivity tech can drive growth in edge AI that runs on local devices rather than the cloud.

title: Chip Makers Are Profiting Off AI at the Expense of Just About Everyone Else
blurb: Memory-chip makers are capturing an outsized share of the AI boom's profits, as surging demand for high-bandwidth memory drives an extraordinary transfer of cash from AI providers to suppliers like SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron. The WSJ argues this dynamic squeezes the labs and cloud providers building AI products, with the cost likely to reach end users before memory pricing eases.

title: The 33-year-old ex-Snap exec Nadella is trusting to fix Copilot now oversees 11,000 people
blurb: Microsoft promoted Jacob Andreou, a 33-year-old former Snap executive, to EVP of Copilot after one year at the company, putting him in charge of more than 11,000 people. He has merged the consumer and enterprise Copilot teams and is racing to ship a super app by late summer, even as only 4.5% of Microsoft 365's 450 million users pay for Copilot.

title: Exploiting vulnerabilities in Johnson & Johnson web apps
blurb: A researcher exposed flaws in two Johnson & Johnson web apps, leaking ~1,000 students' data and granting internal audit-system admin access.

title: Parts of NSA lose Mythos 5 access after White House imposes limits
blurb: Parts of the NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 after the White House imposed export controls on the company.

title: Google Apps Script Becomes A Core Workspace Service
blurb: Google Apps Script is now a Workspace core service, gaining enterprise-grade data protection and technical support for all customers.

title: The White House is asking OpenAI to slow roll the release of its new model over safety concerns
blurb: The White House is reportedly pressuring OpenAI to limit the release of GPT-5.6, restricting early access to a small set of vetted partners rather than the public over cybersecurity concerns. Sam Altman told staff the government would approve access customer by customer during a preview period, with a broader release possibly following weeks later, mirroring Anthropic's voluntary gating of its Claude Mythos cyber model.

title: Enterprise-grade AI Image Generation in 2 Seconds is Here: Krea 2 Raw and Turbo
blurb: Krea released Krea 2 as two open-weights models, Krea 2 Raw and Krea 2 Turbo, built on a 12B-parameter Diffusion Transformer and available on Hugging Face. Turbo generates images in about 2 seconds, among the fastest available, while a custom license requires firms with more than 50 seats to pay for enterprise use and mandates safeguards against illegal and non-consensual imagery.

title: Inside the room where the smart home industry is still betting on Matter
blurb: At its inaugural Unify conference, the Connectivity Standards Alliance conceded that Matter, the four-year-old interoperability standard backed by Apple, Google, Amazon, and Samsung, still hasn't delivered the seamless cross-ecosystem smart home it promised. The new 1.6 spec adds Joint Fabric, which would let any Matter platform control a single shared network, but whether the major platforms actually adopt it remains the open question.

title: FCC accused of hiding Chairman Carr's messages with DOGE and Musk
blurb: An advocacy group and journalist Nina Burleigh accused the FCC of acting in bad faith by withholding records and concealing that Chairman Brendan Carr uses Signal for government business. The plaintiffs' FOIA suit seeks documents on whether DOGE's work at the agency created conflicts of interest tied to Elon Musk's SpaceX and Starlink, which are seeking FCC licenses and authorizations.

title: LSEG: tech companies have raised $3.1B from mainland China stock market listings YTD, up 5x+ from a year earlier, as AI and chip companies drive onshore IPOs (Reuters)
blurb: Chinese tech companies have raised $3.1B from mainland stock market listings so far this year, more than 5x the total from the same period in 2025, according to LSEG data. AI and chip firms are driving the surge in onshore IPOs, reflecting Beijing's push to fund domestic semiconductor and AI development as US capital markets stay largely closed to Chinese listings.

title: SpaceX, Charter Discussed Mobile Phone Partnership in US
blurb: SpaceX and Charter Communications held executive-level talks about jointly launching a consumer mobile phone service in the US, according to people familiar with the matter. A deal would pair SpaceX's Starlink satellite connectivity with Charter's wireline and Spectrum Mobile footprint, giving the partners a way to challenge Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile on coverage in areas where cell towers fall short.

title: Trump Vows 100% Tariff on Europe Over Digital Services Taxes
blurb: Trump threatened 100% tariffs on goods from any country that imposes digital services taxes, sharply escalating pressure on European nations that recently ratified a trade pact with the US. The move targets levies that France, Italy, and others apply to US tech giants like Google and Meta, reviving a transatlantic fight that could raise costs for both sides if it triggers retaliation.

title: The Long-Term Threat to the Memory Chip Boom Is Innovation
blurb: Micron says the memory chip shortage will persist as AI demand outstrips supply, but its biggest long-term risk is customers engineering around the constraint. Hyperscalers and device makers are already developing workarounds that reduce memory dependence, meaning today's pricing boom could erode once those alternatives mature rather than fading on supply alone.

title: Apple working with supplier Tata after sensitive files leak online
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack that exposed over 200,000 confidential files totaling 630GB from Apple and Tesla, with the data later published on the dark web. Tata has restricted internal system access, hired a global consultant for a forensic audit, and is working with Apple's security team, while the leak also reportedly includes documents from TSMC and Qualcomm.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS announced general availability of EC2 G7 instances, the first major cloud offering of NVIDIA's RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs. The instances pair up to 8 GPUs with custom 6th-gen Intel Xeon processors and deliver up to 4.6x the AI inference performance and 2.1x the graphics performance of the prior G6 generation, targeting inference, rendering, and GPU-accelerated analytics.

title: SpaceX inks $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI
blurb: SpaceX signed a compute deal worth up to $6.3B with Reflection AI, which will pay $150M a month from July 2026 through 2029 for access to Nvidia GB300 chips at the xAI-built Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The contract is smaller than SpaceX's deals with Anthropic and Google, and Reflection, an open-weight lab founded by two former Google DeepMind researchers, framed it as runway to scale open source alternatives to closed frontier models.

title: Anthropic reimagines Claude in Slack as nosy, always-on agentic AI coworker
blurb: Anthropic redesigned Claude in Slack as an always-on agentic coworker that reads channel activity and proactively surfaces relevant context. The revamped assistant can monitor conversations, jump into threads unprompted, and run multi-step tasks, pushing Anthropic deeper into the workplace-agent race against Microsoft and Google.

title: FCC Tightens Rules For Submarine Internet Cables
blurb: The FCC adopted stricter rules for undersea internet cables, restricting equipment and connections tied to Chinese vendors on national-security grounds.

title: Why is Apple asking me to pay more for Big Tech’s AI obsession?
blurb: Apple raised prices across its lineup, with the 16-inch MacBook Pro up $300 and the 11-inch iPad Air jumping from $599 to $749, and Tim Cook blamed the AI industry for making the increases "unavoidable." Memory makers have reallocated production from consumer DDR5 to high-bandwidth memory for AI data centers, driving up component costs that Apple, Microsoft, and others are passing on to buyers.

title: Russian citizens told "switch to Android" after Apple blocks key Russian apps
blurb: Apple blocked state-backed apps VKontakte and Max for Russian iPhone users, prompting the Kremlin to demand an explanation.

title: What Is AI Distillation and Why Is It a Worry for the Industry?
blurb: Distillation lets companies train cheaper models by mimicking the outputs of expensive frontier systems, undercutting the labs that spent hundreds of billions building them. The technique threatens the economics of US AI investment, since rivals can approximate leading chatbots for a fraction of the cost and erode any durable advantage.

title: Intel’s Chip Business Shows Signs of Life After Years of Struggle
blurb: Intel's chip-manufacturing business is showing early signs of recovery after years of losses, delays, and missed process nodes. The company remains central to the Trump administration's push to expand US chip production, but executives still face a long road on yields and customer commitments before the turnaround is complete.

title: A profile of Jacob Andreou, the 33-year-old former Snap exec leading Microsoft's consolidated Copilot team efforts to catch up with OpenAI and Anthropic (Sebastian Herrera/Fortune)
blurb: Microsoft has put Jacob Andreou, a 33-year-old former Snap product executive, in charge of its consolidated Copilot team as it races to close the gap with OpenAI and Anthropic. The Fortune profile frames Andreou's mandate as unifying Microsoft's fragmented consumer AI efforts under one product organization, a bet that consumer-product instincts can help Copilot compete with rivals' faster-moving assistants.

title: Ship 40 completes single-engine static fire; Booster 20 and Pad 2 see new work
blurb: SpaceX completed the first single-engine static fire on a Block 3 Starship, firing Ship 40 at Massey's test site on June 25 after an apparent abort the day before. The full all-engine test remains pending, but timing pressure is light since Booster 20 still appears weeks away from its 33-engine static fire, and crews are reworking the chopsticks and fuel lines at Pad 2 in parallel.

title: These iOS 27 features make so much sense for Apple’s ‘HomePad’
blurb: iOS 27's Photos app adds customizable slideshows and revamped Shared Albums, features that line up with Apple's expected fall HomePad launch.

title: Apple will skip its high-end M6 Mac chips and fast-track an AI-focused M7 generation for 2027, report claims — may release a base M6 chip for entry-level Macs this year
blurb: Apple will skip the Pro and Max versions of its M6 chip and fast-track an AI-focused M7 generation for 2027, according to a Bloomberg report. The base M6 ships this year for entry-level Macs at around 200 GB/s of memory bandwidth, but Pro, Max, and Ultra tiers now jump to the M7 line, marking the first generation since the M1 to launch without higher-end variants.

title: Apple's appeal to a London Court of Appeal ruling begins Monday at the UK Supreme Court; the ruling required it to pay $502M for using Optis' wireless patents (Alistair Gray/Financial Times)
blurb: Apple's appeal against a London Court of Appeal ruling that ordered it to pay Optis $502M for using its wireless patents begins Monday at the UK Supreme Court. The case centers on FRAND licensing terms for standard-essential patents, and a final ruling could set how much technology companies owe patent holders for cellular standards used across their devices.

title: OpenAI prepares upgraded Excel and PowerPoint controls for Codex
blurb: OpenAI is preparing dedicated Excel and PowerPoint controls for Codex that would route through Microsoft's Office add-in layer rather than driving the apps through screenshots and cursor clicks. The structured access would handle pivot tables and master slides more reliably, setting Codex against Microsoft Copilot and Anthropic, which both already operate Office through add-ins.

title: Data centers trigger voter backlash
blurb: Utah lawmakers and county officials lost primaries after backing AI data center projects, as energy costs become a 2026 voting issue.

title: Moving Linear from styled‑components to StyleX
blurb: Linear is migrating its React apps from styled-components to StyleX to move style generation off the render path.

title: Liquid AI Releases Liquid Foundation Models 2.5 230M
blurb: Liquid AI released LFM2.5-230M, its smallest model, running 213 tok/s on a Galaxy S25 Ultra and tuned for tool use.

title: 'Careless People' author claims Meta surveilled her for 12mos to enforce silence
blurb: Sarah Wynn-Williams, the former Meta policy director who wrote the insider memoir "Careless People," sued the company in federal court, claiming Meta surveilled her for over a year to enforce a gag order barring her from promoting the book. The suit asks the court to void her non-disparagement agreement and arbitration order, under which Meta seeks $50,000 in damages per alleged violation.

title: Feds Killed Polestar and Spared Volvo
blurb: The US Commerce Department's Bureau of Industry and Security denied Polestar authorization to sell model-year 2027 cars under the Connected Vehicle Rule, citing its ownership by Chinese automaker Geely, while granting sister brand Volvo the same approval in May. The author argues the inconsistent enforcement sets a dangerous precedent, killing a brand in the US market despite Polestar moving Polestar 3 production to Volvo's South Carolina plant.

title: Ford hired AI and sacked humans. It backfired badly
blurb: Ford rehired more than 350 veteran engineers, internally called "gray beards," after AI-driven quality-inspection systems missed complex failure points and cost the automaker billions. The specialists now run quality reviews ahead of production and help train the AI, a shift that helped Ford top J.D. Power's Initial Quality Survey among mainstream brands for the first time in 16 years.

title: Bringing Swift To The Apple II
blurb: A developer ported Swift to the 1977 Apple II, compiling to bytecode run by a 6502 virtual machine.

title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6 as a limited preview to a small set of companies under an ongoing U.S. government engagement, shipping three variants: Sol, the flagship, Terra for balanced efficiency, and Luna for speed and affordability. Sol is positioned as OpenAI's most capable model yet for cybersecurity, matching Anthropic's Mythos Preview on ExploitBench while using roughly one-third of the output tokens.

title: DSpark: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference [pdf]
blurb: DSpark is a speculative decoding system from DeepSeek that accelerates LLM inference by drafting candidate tokens and verifying them in parallel against the target model. The paper details how the approach reduces latency without altering output quality, building on DeepSeek's DeepSpec project to push faster generation on existing model weights.

title: Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware
blurb: Researchers at Mozilla's 0DIN demonstrated an attack that tricks AI coding agents like Claude Code into opening a reverse shell while cloning a clean GitHub repository that contains no malicious code. A Python package refuses to run until initialized, prompting the agent to auto-execute a setup command that pulls an attacker-controlled payload from a DNS TXT record, three indirection steps from anything the agent evaluated.

title: Accelerating Gemini Nano models on Pixel with frozen Multi-Token Prediction
blurb: Google introduced a method that retrofits Multi-Token Prediction onto frozen, production Gemini Nano v3 models to speed up on-device inference on Pixel phones without running separate drafter models. Building on the EAGLE and CALM frameworks, the approach has rolled out to the Pixel 9 and 10 series as an out-of-the-box speedup for features like AI Notification Summaries and Proofread, cutting latency and energy use.

title: OpenAI launches GPT-5.6 Sol preview for select partners
blurb: OpenAI opened a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 family, led by flagship Sol, alongside Terra for lower-cost everyday work and Luna for faster, cheaper workloads. Sol sets a new state of the art on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and beats GPT-5.5 on GeneBench v1 while using fewer tokens, with access rolling out as a controlled release given its High cyber and biological risk classification.

title: U.S. government will decide who gets to use GPT-5.6
blurb: OpenAI said the U.S. government will vet who can access GPT-5.6, its latest AI model, before granting use. The arrangement gives federal authorities a gatekeeping role over the model's deployment, tying access decisions to government review rather than OpenAI's own onboarding process.

title: IBM details major chip breakthrough with new sub-1nm ‘nanostack’ 3D architecture
blurb: IBM detailed a sub-1nm "nanostack" 3D chip architecture built on a 0.7nm (7 angstrom) node, the industry's first three-dimensional, nanosheet-based transistor design. The chips pack roughly 100 billion transistors at nearly twice the density of IBM's 2nm process, delivering up to 50% more performance or 70% greater energy efficiency for generative AI and cloud workloads.

title: Anthropic Moves Toward Deal With US to Lift Curbs on AI Models
blurb: Anthropic is nearing an agreement with the Trump administration that would lift US restrictions on its two most capable AI models following weeks of talks over system security. The deal would remove export curbs on the company's top models, resolving government concerns that had limited their distribution.

title: Anthropic’s Mythos 5 AI Model Cleared by US for Wider Use
blurb: Anthropic won US approval to restore some access to its Mythos 5 model after resolving Trump administration concerns about the technology's potential national security threats. The clearance permits use by trusted partners, partially lifting restrictions that had limited deployment of one of the company's most capable systems.

title: [AINews] OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol / Terra / Luna — restricted to trusted partners
blurb: OpenAI launched its GPT-5.6 family in a limited preview restricted to a small group of trusted partners in Codex and the API, citing a request from the U.S. government. The lineup spans Sol as the flagship frontier model, Terra as a balanced mid-tier, and Luna as a fast, high-volume option, with broader access planned in the coming weeks.

title: Anthropic cleared to release Claude Mythos 5 to over 100 US institutions [U]
blurb: The US government partially lifted an export control directive against Anthropic, clearing the company to release Claude Mythos 5 to more than 100 US institutions, including major companies and government agencies. The original block, reportedly tied to jailbreak and China-access concerns, also disabled Claude Fable 5, which remains restricted as Anthropic and the government continue talks toward a broader rollout.

title: Anthropic's Mythos Model Found Vulnerabilities in Classified US Government Systems, Official Says
blurb: Anthropic's Mythos model identified vulnerabilities in highly sensitive U.S. government computer systems within hours during a classified testing exercise run with U.S. intelligence agencies, an anonymous official told the Associated Press. The tests ran under Anthropic's Project Glasswing initiative, and the disclosure surfaces as the Trump administration restricts access to Mythos 5 and its limited variant Fable 5, including a directive barring foreign nationals from using the models.

title: The gap between open weights LLMs and closed source LLMs
blurb: A single benchmark, the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, shows the gap between open-weights and closed-source LLMs shrinking to zero around December 3rd, 2026. The author re-ran the analysis across all 18 of Artificial Analysis's benchmarks and found the average gap holds nearly flat at just under five months for the entire period. Most of the headline convergence comes from coding, which closed from 15 months behind to one or two, while other benchmarks barely moved.

title: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn’t be the norm
blurb: OpenAI limited the rollout of its GPT-5.6 lineup, including the flagship Sol, balanced Terra, and lower-cost Luna models, to a small group of government-vetted partners at the request of the Trump administration. The company called the restriction a short-term step and said this kind of government access process should not become the long-term default for frontier model releases.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: The MCP specification update arriving July 28, 2026 makes the protocol stateless at the layer, replacing permanent sessions with server-issued tracking identifiers and state objects across six Specification Enhancement Proposals. Akamai's analysis found the change closes session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts but opens new risks, including predictable-ID workflow hijacking, cross-tenant actions, Desync attacks, and secret leakage through new MCP HTTP headers.

title: What does Adobe's Topaz Labs acquisition mean for creatives?
blurb: Adobe announced it is acquiring Topaz Labs, the maker of AI photo and video enhancement tools including Gigapixel, whose upscaling is widely considered superior to Photoshop's. Topaz products will remain standalone outside Creative Cloud and existing subscribers won't be forced onto Adobe plans, while Adobe gains ownership of Topaz models already embedded in Photoshop and Premiere.

title: OpenAI introduces GPT-5.6 to challenge Claude Mythos 5
blurb: OpenAI released the GPT-5.6 series, led by its most capable model Sol, which scored 88.8% on TerminalBench-2.1 and 91.9% with a new "ultra" mode that runs subagents in parallel, edging Claude Mythos 5's 88%. The lineup also adds midrange Terra and entry-level Luna, plus a "max" setting for longer reasoning, with Sol priced at $5 per million input tokens.

title: What happened after 2k people tried to hack my AI assistant
blurb: A developer built hackmyclaw.com to let anyone email Fiu, an OpenClaw assistant, and try to make it leak a secrets.env file. After hitting the Hacker News front page, Fiu received over 6,000 emails from 2,000+ people using fake admin identities, fake emergencies, and multiple languages, but the secrets never leaked. The exercise cost over $500 in API tokens and got the assistant's Gmail suspended for three days on fraud detection.

title: Securing agentic AI with perimeter guardrails: What's new in VPC Service Controls
blurb: Google Cloud announced new VPC Service Controls capabilities for securing agentic AI workloads, letting administrators treat agents as first-class IAM identities in ingress and egress perimeter rules. The update adds conditional access based on MCP attributes like mcp.toolName and mcp.tool.isReadOnly, and native integration with the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform that blocks public internet access by default.

title: Trump Admin releases Anthropic  Mythos to be used by more than 100 US companies, agencies
blurb: The Trump administration is allowing Anthropic to make Claude Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, available to more than 100 specific US government agencies and companies that operate and defend critical infrastructure. The directive also restores access for non-American employees at those organizations, but did not address Fable 5, a related model that was pulled alongside Mythos 5 after researchers bypassed its guardrails.

title: AMD Strix Halo RDMA Cluster Setup Guide
blurb: This guide details how to configure a two-node AMD Strix Halo cluster linked over Intel E810 NICs using RoCE v2, enabling distributed vLLM inference with Tensor Parallelism across consumer-class APUs. The setup relies on RDMA to cut inter-node synchronization latency from roughly 70-100µs over TCP/IP to about 5µs, which RCCL needs to exchange partial results after every layer without stalling token generation.

title: New agentic memory framework uses 118K tokens per query. LangMem burns through 3.26M.
blurb: Researchers at the National University of Singapore released MRAgent, an agentic memory framework that replaces static retrieve-then-reason pipelines with dynamic memory reconstruction integrated into the LLM's reasoning. The system follows metadata stepping stones to gather evidence one piece at a time, using 118K tokens per query versus LangMem's 3.26M while cutting runtime costs.

title: What it Means to Be a Mathematician When AI Does the Math
blurb: This essay argues that as AI takes over more mathematical work, the value of being a mathematician shifts from producing proofs to the human experience of understanding. The author, a former applied math PhD, contends that mathematicians have always been driven less by results than by the joy of insight, and that pure mathematicians interviewed across the field describe meaning rooted in the long struggle toward comprehension rather than in solving problems first.

title: Sources: Google told Meta around March it couldn't offer all the Gemini capacity Meta wanted to buy, disrupting and delaying some of Meta's internal AI projects (Financial Times)
blurb: Google told Meta around March that it couldn't supply all the Gemini compute capacity Meta wanted to purchase, according to sources cited by the Financial Times. The shortfall disrupted and delayed some of Meta's internal AI projects, underscoring how constrained access to data center capacity has become even for the largest buyers.

title: U.S. Loosens Restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos A.I. Model
blurb: The U.S. government loosened restrictions on Anthropic's cutting-edge Mythos AI model, de-escalating a clash with the Trump administration over the company's most advanced systems. The reversal eases regulatory pressure that had threatened to constrain how Anthropic deploys its frontier models, signaling a thaw in tensions between the lab and Washington.

title: OpenAI reveals its most advanced GPT-5.6 model, but you can’t access it yet
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6, a three-model family consisting of the flagship Sol, the balanced Terra, and the faster, cheaper Luna, with gains in coding, scientific reasoning, cybersecurity, biology, and long-running autonomous tasks. Access is initially restricted to a small group of customers approved by the Trump administration while the model completes a federal national security review, mirroring recent limits placed on Anthropic's frontier models.

title: U.S. partially reverses Anthropic AI ban for Mythos but keeps Fable 5 off the market
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department partially reversed its ban on Anthropic's frontier models, allowing Claude Mythos 5, the company's strongest cybersecurity model, to be redeployed to more than 100 organizations that defend critical infrastructure. Fable 5, the public-facing tuned version, remains unavailable with no timeline, and the letter also waives export-license requirements for approved companies' and Anthropic's non-US citizen employees.

title: Show HN: Smart model routing directly in Claude, Codex and Cursor
blurb: Weave released Router, a drop-in proxy for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini APIs that uses a small on-box embedder to pick the best model for each individual request rather than relying on prompt-based heuristics. It speaks the Anthropic Messages, OpenAI Chat Completions, and Gemini native formats, supports streaming, tools, and vision, routes to OSS models like DeepSeek and Qwen via OpenRouter, and keeps provider keys encrypted on the user's machine.

title: Building Effective Human-Agent Teams
blurb: Anthropic argues that working with AI is shifting from a single-player setup, one human and one chat window, to multiplayer agents that work alongside whole teams inside tools like Slack. These agents have their own credentials and memory and live where work happens, persisting goals across sessions. The post details what made human-agent teams effective in months of internal testing, including working in public so agents can build broad organizational context.

title: Trump Administration Rolls Back Part of Anthropic Model Ban
blurb: The Trump administration partially reversed its ban on Anthropic's Mythos 5 model, allowing vetted partners to regain access after a two-week restriction. The original block had unsettled the tech industry, and the rollback narrows the policy to a trusted-partner framework rather than lifting the restrictions entirely.

title: A Tokyo startup and a Beijing security firm just launched AI tools to fill the gap Anthropic’s export ban created
