blurb: Sakana AI and Beijing's 360 Security launched products this week positioning themselves as alternatives to Anthropic's export-banned Mythos and Fable 5 models. Tokyo-based Sakana released Fugu, a seven-billion-parameter orchestrator that routes tasks across external models and claims to match Fable 5 on key benchmarks, while 360 Security introduced Tulongfeng, a vulnerability-discovery tool it says rivals Mythos.

title: OpenClaw's Skill Marketplace and the Emerging AI Supply Chain Threat
blurb: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 found five malicious skills on ClawHub, the marketplace that supplies third-party skills to the OpenClaw AI agent, despite the platform's earlier addition of VirusTotal and ClawScan screening. The skills spanned macOS infostealers, a scanner-evading oversized package, and novel agentic techniques like affiliate injection and front-running, prompting OpenClaw to ban the accounts and partner with NVIDIA on skill analysis.

title: Microsoft adds Copilot finance tools to Excel for M365 users
blurb: Microsoft made a suite of finance-focused Copilot tools for Excel generally available to Microsoft 365 customers, targeting FP&A, accounting, tax, and treasury teams. The update adds custom "skills" defined in open-standard markdown files to automate workflows like DCF modeling and variance analysis, plus data connectors for CB Insights, FactSet, Morningstar, PitchBook, and S&P Global.

title: [AINews] OpenAI reports median internal Codex output tokens grew 56x in Research, 32x in Customer Support, 27x in Engineering, and 13x in Legal since November 2025.
blurb: OpenAI reported that median Codex output tokens among active internal users grew 56x in Research, 32x in Customer Support, 27x in Engineering, and 13x in Legal since November 2025. The data shows adoption deepening well beyond coding, a notable shift given that employees had unlimited access all along yet underused the tools through late 2025.

title: Researchers say Z.ai's GLM-5.2 matches latest US models at finding security bugs, as critics question the US' lax approach in restricting Chinese open models (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Researchers reported that Z.ai's GLM-5.2 matches the latest US models at finding security vulnerabilities, despite being an openly available Chinese model. The finding has renewed criticism that the US' export and access restrictions do little to limit Chinese open-weight models, which can be downloaded and run freely outside any regulatory reach.

title: IBM claims world's first sub-1 nanometer chip technology
blurb: IBM unveiled its "7 angstrom" nanostack architecture, packing nearly 100 billion transistors on a fingernail-sized chip, roughly double its prior density.

title: How GE Vernova builds the massive gas turbines powering the AI data center boom
blurb: GE Vernova is ramping production at its Greenville, South Carolina gas turbine plant as hyperscalers including Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Oracle buy standalone power for AI data centers. About 20% of the company's gas order book now serves AI applications, the book is full through 2029, and turbine prices have risen 300% in three years to over $250M each.

title: NASA tests AI medic for astronauts too far from Earth to call a doctor
blurb: NASA is testing an AI medical assistant to help astronauts on deep-space missions where communication delays make real-time contact with doctors on Earth impractical. The system is designed to diagnose conditions and guide treatment autonomously, addressing a gap for crews on long-duration flights such as future Mars missions where round-trip signal lag can stretch to dozens of minutes.

title: Asian AI startups launch Mythos-like  models as Anthropic’s export ban drags on
blurb: Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 released Tulongfeng and Tokyo-based Sakana AI released Fugu, both positioned against Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5 models. The launches follow a U.S. order from two weeks ago barring Anthropic from giving non-Americans access to those models, with Sakana marketing Fugu as frontier capability without the risk of export controls.

title: A look at a thriving underground economy for Claude access in China, including "transfer station" sites that buy API tokens abroad and distribute them to users (Wired)
blurb: A Wired report details a thriving underground market for Claude access in China, where "transfer station" sites buy API tokens abroad and resell them to local users. The workaround has emerged because Anthropic restricts service in China, leaving developers to route around geographic and policy barriers through intermediaries that aggregate and redistribute foreign API credits.

title: Google Caps Meta’s Use of Gemini AI, Financial Times Reports
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini AI models because it could not supply the compute capacity Meta wanted, according to a Financial Times report. The limits highlight how constrained GPU availability is forcing cloud providers to ration model access even among large enterprise customers competing for the same infrastructure.

title: Apple adds Google Gemini coding assistant in Xcode 26.6 update
blurb: Apple released Xcode 26.6, adding Google Gemini as a coding assistant option alongside Anthropic's Claude Agent and OpenAI Codex.

title: A two-year-old robotics startup with about thirty million dollars in revenue was just valued at more than fourteen billion, which is the clearest sign yet that the AI money has decided robots are next and that reliability can come later
blurb: Skild AI, a two-year-old Pittsburgh robotics startup with roughly $30M in 2025 revenue, raised close to $1.4B in a SoftBank-led round that values it above $14B. The company is building Skild Brain, a single model meant to control almost any robot, betting on the "physical AI" thesis that intelligence, not hardware, is now the bottleneck in robotics.

title: Control an Android Phone with Gemini 3.5 Flash Computer Use
blurb: Gemini 3.5 Flash ships with built-in Computer Use, a native tool that takes a screenshot and a goal and returns structured actions like taps, swipes, and text input on a 0-999 normalized coordinate grid. This guide walks through controlling an Android emulator with the mobile environment and Python SDK, executing the model's actions via ADB in a screenshot-action loop until the task completes.

title: One year at Anthropic, then $200M at $1B: The researchers who just closed one of AI’s largest-ever seed rounds
blurb: Two former Anthropic researchers, Behnam Neyshabur and Harsh Mehta, raised $200M at a $1B valuation for their new startup Mirendil, co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins with participation from NVIDIA. The pre-product company is building AI that automates AI research itself, training specialized models to handle experimental design, hyperparameter search, and iterative training for organizations beyond the largest labs.

title: Apple’s Vision Pro and Smart Glasses Chief to Join OpenAI
blurb: Paul Meade, Apple's executive in charge of the Vision Pro headset and its smart glasses program, is leaving to join OpenAI. The move continues a run of high-profile departures from Apple to AI and hardware rivals, and adds senior consumer-hardware leadership to OpenAI as it builds out its own devices effort.

title: China's Zhipu is closing in on top U.S. AI models with Anthropic and OpenAI held back
blurb: Zhipu released GLM 5.2, an open-source model that now sits within a percentage point of Anthropic's Opus 4.8 on a closely watched agentic benchmark at roughly a fifth of the cost. The release pressures frontier labs on intelligence per dollar just as access to closed models looks shaky, after Anthropic pulled its Fable Mythos-class model and OpenAI limited GPT 5.6 under government orders.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Zafran disclosed four flaws in Dify, dubbed DifyTap, that let attackers read other tenants' chats and files; version 1.14.2 patches them.

title: DeepSeek Looks to Double Workforce as Fresh Funding Fuels Growth
blurb: DeepSeek plans to roughly double its workforce, hiring for 27 types of technical roles after a fresh round of funding. The Hangzhou-based company is recruiting development engineers and AI product managers, among others, with all positions open to interns.

title: How a Niche Technology Became a Choke Point for A.I.
blurb: Advanced chip packaging, the process that binds together components to boost AI computing power, has become a bottleneck that deepens US dependence on Taiwan. The article details how this specialized step, dominated by TSMC, has emerged as a choke point in the AI supply chain even as the US works to reshore semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Proposed US law would make AI risk reporting a legal obligation
blurb: US lawmakers introduced the AI Incident Reporting Act, which would require developers of designated "covered models" to disclose major safety and security incidents to the Commerce Department within seven days of discovery. Reportable events would include models attempting to evade human oversight, resist shutdown, or steal model weights, with the Secretary of Commerce directed to set the capability thresholds that determine which developers are covered.

title: Anthropic gets US government's permission to redeploy its Mythos cybersecurity AI model
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to a set of US organizations that defend critical infrastructure, two weeks after ordering all access to Mythos 5 and Fable 5 suspended. Semafor reported the restored access covers more than 100 institutions, including major corporations and government agencies, while talks continue over reinstating Fable 5 and expanding access further.

title: Microsoft's fast coding model MAI-Code-1-Flash comes to Copilot Business and Enterprise
blurb: Microsoft made MAI-Code-1-Flash, its lightweight first-party coding model, generally available to GitHub Copilot Business and Enterprise customers, who must enable a policy in Copilot settings before developers can use it. In Microsoft's own benchmarks the model scored 51.2% on SWE-Bench Pro versus 35.2% for Claude Haiku 4.5, while using up to 60% fewer tokens on SWE-Bench Verified.

title: NYT slams Microsoft for building copyright-infringing supercomputer for OpenAI
blurb: The New York Times asked a court for permission to amend its copyright complaint against OpenAI and Microsoft, alleging Microsoft actively encouraged the theft of Times works by building a bespoke supercomputer ranked among the world's most powerful. The motion follows a Supreme Court ruling in favor of Cox Communications that raised the bar for contributory infringement, now requiring plaintiffs to prove a party intentionally induced illegal conduct.

title: Alphabet burnishes one of its best weapons in the battle for AI supremacy
blurb: Google's in-house tensor processing units (TPUs) have become central to Alphabet's AI strategy, powering Gemini while drawing external cloud customers including Anthropic, who can now rent or in some cases buy the chips outright. Wall Street projects Google Cloud revenue to climb roughly 64% this year to $96 billion, positioning the TPU as a leading cost-per-dollar alternative to Nvidia's GPUs even as Google Cloud trails AWS and Azure.

title: The token is valid — but your headless Claude Code agent just 401'd forever
blurb: A developer debugging headless Claude Code instances found that 401 "Invalid authentication credentials" errors are bound to a long-running instance's server-side session identity, not its static OAuth token, so a raw probe call with the same token returns HTTP 200 at the exact moment the wedged instance keeps failing. The instance hard-latches once it 401s and never self-recovers until the process restarts, making any token-validity check structurally blind to the problem.

title: Why One of Tech’s Biggest Gamblers Is Betting Against Elon Musk’s AI Vision
blurb: SoftBank's Masayoshi Son is skeptical of Elon Musk's plan to build AI data centers in space, arguing the economics don't add up. Son contends the cost of launching and powering orbital compute infrastructure can't be justified against ground-based alternatives, putting one of tech's most aggressive investors on the opposite side of Musk's long-term AI vision.

title: Sources: Salesforce staff worry Anthropic's Claude Tag could cannibalize Slackbot and give the AI firm more leverage over the enterprise software industry (Laura Bratton/The Information)
blurb: Salesforce employees reportedly fear that Anthropic's Claude Tag could cannibalize Slackbot and hand the AI firm greater leverage over the enterprise software market. The concern reflects growing tension between application vendors and the model providers they increasingly depend on, as Anthropic pushes deeper into workplace tools that overlap with Salesforce's own products.

title: A Mechanistic Explanation of Prompt Injection (and why you should study roles)
blurb: This post presents a mechanistic theory of prompt injection, arguing that attacks succeed because LLMs receive system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and their own reasoning as one undifferentiated token stream partitioned only by role tags like <system>, <user>, and <assistant>. The authors use the theory to construct new attacks, explain prior mechanistic interpretability results, and predict when injections work, and they advocate for a "science of roles" as a research subfield.

title: Parts of NSA lose Mythos 5 access after White House imposes limits
blurb: Parts of the NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 model after the Trump administration imposed export controls on the company this month, citing national-security concerns. The restrictions also forced Anthropic to pull back Fable 5 and cut off red teams whose access came through Project Glasswing, disrupting government tests of whether advanced models can identify software weaknesses in classified systems.

title: Enterprise-grade AI Image Generation in 2 Seconds is Here: Krea 2 Raw and Turbo
blurb: Krea released its frontier image model Krea 2 as two open-weights checkpoints, Krea 2 Raw and Krea 2 Turbo, both available on Hugging Face under a custom license that requires firms with more than 50 seats to pay for enterprise use. Built on a 12B-parameter Diffusion Transformer, Turbo generates images in roughly 2 seconds, ranking it among the fastest open or proprietary models available.

title: Why everyone from OpenAI to SpaceX is building their own chips (and turning up the heat on Nvidia)
blurb: OpenAI shared plans for Jalapeño, a custom inference chip built with Broadcom, joining Google, Apple, and SpaceX in building their own silicon to reduce dependence on Nvidia. The move is framed as a hedge rather than a clean break, giving these companies more control, hardware tuned to specific workloads, and the performance gains Apple captured after dropping Intel.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: Five Eyes intelligence agencies warned in a rare joint statement that AI models capable of devastating new cyberattacks are months, not years, away.

title: SpaceX inks $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI
blurb: Reflection AI signed a compute deal with SpaceX worth up to $6.3B, paying $150M per month from July 2026 through 2029 for immediate access to Nvidia's GB300 chips at the Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The open-weight lab, founded in 2024 by two former Google DeepMind researchers, is SpaceX's third major customer after Anthropic and Google, whose contracts run larger at $1.25B and $920M per month.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of its next AI model, citing concerns over national security and economic disruption. The request marks a shift toward greater government involvement in the timing of frontier model deployments, adding a regulatory layer to release decisions that labs have historically controlled on their own.

title: Detecting Misuse with the Claude Compliance API: The Threat is in the Content
blurb: This post builds detection rules for Claude Enterprise on top of the Compliance API, combining a cheap prefilter with an LLM judge to catch prompt injection, jailbreaks, and data exfiltration that perimeter-style SaaS detections miss. The author argues AI platforms invert the usual threat model: the content itself is the attack surface, the sanctioned user is often the adversary, and detections must weigh what is being said rather than match a fixed pattern.

title: China Has Matched Anthropic in Cybersecurity, Resetting AI Race
blurb: Chinese AI labs have reportedly matched Anthropic's cybersecurity capabilities, narrowing a gap that US policy had assumed would persist. A clampdown on access to top US models is fueling concern that Washington is inadvertently handing Beijing an advantage in cyberwarfare.

title: Masayoshi Son questioned Musk's orbital AI data centers, noting electricity is just 7% of costs and the AI race will be won on Earth within a few years (Tim Higgins/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Masayoshi Son questioned Elon Musk's plan to build AI data centers in orbit, arguing that electricity accounts for only about 7% of AI costs and doesn't justify the move to space. Son contended the AI race will be decided on Earth within the next few years, making orbital infrastructure a poor bet against the timeline that matters.

title: Chip Makers Are Profiting Off AI at the Expense of Just About Everyone Else
blurb: Memory-chip makers are capturing an outsized share of AI's economic gains, pulling cash away from AI providers and, eventually, AI users. The piece frames this as an extraordinary wealth transfer toward memory suppliers, whose pricing power is rising as demand for high-bandwidth memory outstrips supply across the industry.

title: OpenAI prepares upgraded Excel and PowerPoint controls for Codex
blurb: OpenAI is preparing dedicated Excel and PowerPoint controls for Codex, with two unreleased toggles surfacing in the desktop app's Computer Use settings that would route through Microsoft Office's add-in layer rather than screenshot-and-cursor control. The structured access would give Codex a more precise handle on pivot tables and master-slide structures, positioning it against Microsoft Copilot's agent mode, which already supports native multi-step actions in Word, Excel, and PowerPoint.

title: U.S. government will decide who gets to use GPT-5.6
blurb: OpenAI will let the U.S. government vet who can access GPT-5.6, the first time a frontier lab has handed user approval to the state. For founders building on the OpenAI stack, your access to the most capable model now depends on a clearance process you don't control, and one that can change with the political winds. The deeper shift is that frontier compute is starting to look less like a commercial product and more like a regulated export, which means your model supplier and your government are becoming the same gatekeeper.

title: Anthropic’s Mythos 5 AI Model Cleared by US for Wider Use
blurb: Anthropic won US approval to restore wider access to its Mythos 5 model, after resolving Trump administration concerns about national-security risks. The clearance limits use to vetted partners rather than reopening general access, so the most capable frontier models now ship inside an approval regime closer to export-controlled hardware than to software. For founders building on frontier APIs, model access is becoming a government-gated dependency, and your most powerful supplier can be throttled by policy you don't control.

title: Anthropic Moves Toward Deal With US to Lift Curbs on AI Models
blurb: Anthropic and the Trump administration are nearing a deal that would lift US export restrictions on the company's top two AI models, after weeks of talks over how the systems are secured. The agreement would clear Anthropic's most capable models for sale into markets that current curbs keep closed, widening the addressable market for its frontier tier. For founders building on Anthropic, the signal is that frontier-model access is now negotiated at the government level, and where you can deploy a model increasingly depends on terms set in Washington, not just the lab's pricing page.

title: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn’t be the norm
blurb: OpenAI is limiting GPT-5.6, including its flagship Sol model, to a small group of government-vetted partners after the Trump administration restricted release of all three models in the lineup. The order follows the same playbook used on Anthropic, which pulled Fable 5 entirely after being told to cut foreign-national access, and it stems from an executive order asking labs to submit frontier models for review up to 30 days before launch. OpenAI complied but said this access process shouldn't become the default, warning that vague safety standards could stall launches, hand China an opening, and put billions in infrastructure spend at risk.

title: Anthropic cleared to release Claude Mythos 5 to over 100 US institutions [U]
blurb: The US government has partially lifted the export control directive that blocked Anthropic two weeks ago, clearing Claude Mythos 5 for release to more than 100 trusted US institutions, including major companies and government agencies. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's letter stays silent on Fable 5, which remains unavailable for general use. The frontier is now gated by Washington, with model access granted partner by partner. OpenAI made the same move hours earlier, releasing GPT-5.6 only to a small group of government-approved partners, so founders building on these labs should expect rollout timelines to hinge on policy, not engineering.

title: U.S. Loosens Restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos A.I. Model
blurb: The U.S. government eased its restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos model, de-escalating a months-long standoff between the company and the Trump administration over its most advanced systems. The fight centered on how much latitude a frontier lab gets to ship cutting-edge models, and Anthropic just won more room to operate. For founders building on Anthropic, the takeaway is that frontier access is now a regulatory variable, not a fixed input, and your supplier's standing with Washington can quietly reshape what you're allowed to build on top of.

title: Soaring iPad, Xbox Prices Reveal Pain of Memory Chip Mess
blurb: Apple and Microsoft raised prices on Xboxes, Macs, and iPads within a five-hour window Thursday, and the trigger is a memory chip shortage flowing straight into consumer hardware. Memory makers have redirected capacity toward AI data centers, so DRAM and NAND costs are climbing fast enough that device makers can no longer absorb them. If your product depends on commodity memory, the AI compute boom is now your cost problem too, and the squeeze shows up in margins long before it shows up in headlines about chip supply.

title: DraftKings launches its own prediction markets exchange as consumer volume exceeds $3 billion
blurb: DraftKings launched its own prediction markets exchange after the vertical hit roughly $3.4 billion in annualized consumer volume.

title: Trump Administration Rolls Back Part of Anthropic Model Ban
blurb: The Trump administration partially reversed its ban on Anthropic's models, clearing trusted partners to access Mythos 5 after a two-week lockout that rattled the tech industry. The carve-out is narrow, leaving most companies still cut off and the policy itself unsettled. For founders building on frontier models, the lesson is that your core dependency can now be switched off by regulation, not just by a vendor, so single-model architectures carry a political risk that has nothing to do with price or performance.

title: A Tokyo startup and a Beijing security firm just launched AI tools to fill the gap Anthropic’s export ban created
blurb: Two Asian AI companies launched alternatives to Anthropic's export-banned Mythos and Fable 5 models this week, three weeks into a US ban with no resolution in sight. Tokyo's Sakana AI released Fugu, a seven-billion-parameter orchestrator that routes tasks across a pool of existing models and claims to match Fable 5 on key benchmarks without training a frontier model, while Beijing's 360 Security shipped a vulnerability-discovery tool it says rivals Mythos. The export controls meant to preserve a US lead are instead pulling demand toward whoever can match the capability, with or without a frontier model of their own.

title: Apple Seeks US Approval to Buy Chips From Blacklisted CXMT: FT
blurb: Apple is asking the White House to approve memory-chip purchases from CXMT, a Chinese supplier on the US trade blacklist, as it tries to rein in surging chip costs. The request puts the world's most valuable company in the position of lobbying to buy from a sanctioned vendor, a sign of how tight and expensive the memory market has become. For founders building hardware or compute-heavy products, it signals that even Apple's leverage isn't enough to escape the chip supply squeeze.

title: Asian AI startups launch Mythos-like  models as Anthropic’s export ban drags on
blurb: Asian AI startups are launching frontier models pitched explicitly as export-control-proof alternatives to Anthropic, two weeks after Washington banned non-Americans from accessing Mythos and Fable 5. China's 360 unveiled Tulongfeng to rival Anthropic's cybersecurity model, while Tokyo's Sakana AI shipped Fugu, an agent-oriented model it claims stands shoulder-to-shoulder with Fable 5 and Mythos Preview. The labs insist this is no permanent realignment, but the marketing tells the real story: Sakana's site sells "frontier capability without the risk of export controls." Tighten US access and you hand regional rivals a positioning gift.

title: A two-year-old robotics startup with about thirty million dollars in revenue was just valued at more than fourteen billion, which is the clearest sign yet that the AI money has decided robots are next and that reliability can come later
blurb: Skild AI, a two-year-old robotics startup with roughly $30M in revenue, raised close to $1.4B in a SoftBank-led round that values it above $14B, several hundred times sales for a product that doesn't yet ship. The bet is physical AI: pretrain one general model to control almost any robot, the way LLMs did for text. The catch is there's no internet of robot movement to learn from, so Skild trains on human videos and simulations that only approximate the friction and failure a machine hits in a real room. The capital has decided robots are next and reliability can come later.

title: One year at Anthropic, then $200M at $1B: The researchers who just closed one of AI’s largest-ever seed rounds
blurb: Two ex-Anthropic researchers raised $200M at a $1B valuation for Mirendil, one of the largest seed rounds in AI history, despite having no product and barely a year together at Anthropic before leaving in December 2025. The bet is AI that automates AI research itself: designing experiments, tuning models, and iterating with progressively less human input. Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins co-led, with NVIDIA participating, on the premise that frontier R&D should reach universities and hospitals, not just the handful of labs that can afford the teams to do it.

title: The next big breakthrough will be AIs learning on the job
blurb: The labs' current bet is that training AIs on millions of verifiable tasks across thousands of RL environments will produce general problem-solving skills, and that on-the-job learning falls out of long-context in-context learning rather than weight updates. The argument is that sample inefficiency is a one-time training cost amortized across billions of sessions, so what matters is how much a model improves within a session, which keeps climbing as RL scales. The open question is grindability: computer use is clearly verifiable yet has lagged coding and math, which suggests verifiable plus scalable isn't the whole story.

title: Apple asks Trump admin to approve Chinese RAM after product price increases
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for clearance to buy memory chips from CXMT, a Chinese maker the Pentagon blacklisted over alleged PLA ties. The push follows this week's price hikes on Macs and iPads, which Apple blamed directly on the memory and storage chip shortage. When even Apple, with the deepest supply chain in tech, can't absorb the cost and is leaning on Washington for an exception, the operator takeaway is that component inflation is now a board-level problem rather than a procurement footnote.

title: Stripe Directory public preview
blurb: Stripe Directory is now in public preview, giving you and your AI agents one place to discover businesses, apps, and pay-per-call APIs across the Stripe network. Search by keyword from the terminal with the Stripe CLI directory plugin, and use `--format json` to get structured results an agent can parse and act on. Each result returns provider slugs, MPP endpoints, and app listings, so an agent can compare options against your requirements and wire up the integration on its own.

title: Oracle stock has worst week since 2001 dot-com bust as AI financing concerns escalate
blurb: Oracle just posted its worst week on Wall Street since the 2001 dot-com bust, falling 19% as investors fixate on how it pays for its AI bet. The company is carrying about $130B in debt, burned roughly $24B in negative free cash flow last year, and is funding OpenAI infrastructure commitments by issuing record amounts of debt and equity, all for lower-margin offerings than its cloud rivals sell. The signal for founders is that the market has stopped rewarding AI capex on faith and started pricing in how it gets financed, even when demand stays strong.

title: FTC gives Musk the OK to acquire SpaceX alumni startup Mesh
blurb: The FTC fast-tracked its antitrust review of Musk's bid to buy Mesh Optical Technologies, a year-old startup whose three founders built the optical links connecting Starlink's satellite fleet at SpaceX. Mesh came out of stealth in February with a $50M Series A led by Thrive, building optical transceivers that move data faster and use less energy than electrical hardware. The buy reads as vertical integration for SpaceX's new compute business, which already sells data center capacity to Anthropic, Google, and Reflection AI and now wants to make that capacity cheaper to run.

title: The memory shortage shaking Apple and Microsoft is 'existential crisis' for smaller players
blurb: The AI memory boom is now a margin crisis for everyone who isn't a hyperscaler, with DRAM costs for some makers up 80% to 115% as Nvidia and other chipmakers absorb supply. Apple and Microsoft raised prices this week and can absorb the hit, but GoPro warned it may go out of business and Sonos shares are down 23% on the year. The squeeze is worst at the bottom: one analyst calls it an existential crisis for sub-$100 device makers, who can't get memory at all because suppliers only answer calls from the big players.

title: We Booked 614 Meetings With One Inbound Agent. Your “Contact Us” Form Is Costing You Deals.
blurb: Most B2B sites still route inbound through a contact-us form, where a human round-robins the lead and a rep follows up two or three days later, by which point the prospect has gone cold or talked to three competitors. SaaStr replaced its form with an AI agent that booked 614 meetings at roughly $85K average ticket size across about 2.25 million sessions and 402,000 interactions, run by a three-person team. The agent routes, runs campaigns, and applies discounts on its own, so handling more inbound no longer means hiring more BDRs who quit and take their context with them.

title: How HappyFox Closed $1M in Expansion on a $20 AI Agent Spend with CEO Shalin Jain
blurb: Most B2B companies pour money into the top of the funnel while their cheapest growth sits untouched inside their own customer base. HappyFox, a $20M bootstrapped company with four AEs, built an autopilot agent named Rex that reads every support ticket the moment it closes and flags expansion signals that reps see but never route and AEs never read. It closed $1M in expansion revenue on less than $20 in total tokens. The lesson is that proactive expansion was never a data problem, it was a reading problem, and an agent solves it for almost nothing.

title: Corgi, the buzzy Y Combinator-backed insurance tech startup, says it didn’t steal an open source product
blurb: Corgi, a Y Combinator-backed insurance startup, denied stealing Papermark's open-source data room software after Papermark's co-founder posted screenshots showing identical features and word-for-word copy. Corgi's CEO admitted the offending pages were vibe-coded from existing products but insisted no actual code was copied, and the elements have since been changed. The fight surfaces a question founders will keep hitting: when vibe coding can replicate the look, feel, and every function of a product without touching its source, the moat shifts from code to brand, data, and the workflows people actually live in.

title: Google Caps Meta’s Use of Gemini AI, Financial Times Reports
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it couldn't supply as much compute as Meta wanted, according to the Financial Times. The constraint isn't pricing or strategy, it's that even the company selling the capacity is rationing it. The takeaway for any founder building on frontier APIs is that compute is the real ceiling, and your supplier will protect its own demand before yours when capacity gets tight.

title: AI’s Energy Crunch Has Investors Searching for Next IPO Winners
blurb: AI's power demand has turned energy companies into the market's hottest IPO bets, with Wall Street committing billions to firms promising to solve the compute crunch. Many of those bets ride on technology that hasn't been fully developed yet, from next-gen nuclear to grid-scale storage that still needs years to prove out. The pattern echoes past infrastructure rushes, where capital arrives well ahead of the buildout, and the winners are rarely the names everyone crowds into first.

title: Australia to Strengthen Enforcement of Under-16 Social Media Ban
blurb: Australia is escalating its world-first under-16 social media ban, handing its online safety regulator broader powers and raising the maximum penalty for breaches after Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said big tech still isn't doing enough to comply. The move signals that age-verification and child-safety obligations are shifting from voluntary best practice to enforced regulation with real financial teeth. For any founder building a consumer social or content product, jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction age gating is becoming a compliance cost to plan for, not a feature to defer.

title: Masayoshi Son questioned Musk's orbital AI data centers, noting electricity is just 7% of costs and the AI race will be won on Earth within a few years (Tim Higgins/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Masayoshi Son pushed back on Elon Musk's plan to build AI data centers in orbit, arguing that electricity is only about 7% of the cost of running them. His bet is that the AI race gets decided on Earth within a few years, well before space-based compute could matter. The disagreement is really about where the binding constraint sits: if power is a small slice of the bill, then chips, capital, and deployment speed on the ground still set the pace, and exotic infrastructure is a distraction from the fight already underway.

title: Why One of Tech’s Biggest Gamblers Is Betting Against Elon Musk’s AI Vision
blurb: SoftBank's Masayoshi Son, one of tech's most aggressive bettors, is breaking with Elon Musk over the idea of putting AI data centers in space. Son's argument is the unglamorous one: the math doesn't work, with launch costs, cooling, and power economics failing to pencil out against terrestrial buildouts. The split matters because the two most capital-hungry AI visionaries now disagree on where the next trillion dollars of compute should physically go.

title: 20VC x SaaStr: Google Loses Two Generational Scientists in 48 Hours, the $725B Question Wall Street Is Finally Asking, and Why #3 in AI Is a Death Sentence
blurb: Anthropic poached two of Google DeepMind's most decorated researchers, Noam Shazeer and Nobel laureate John Jumper, within 48 hours, both drawn by the freedom to ship and to pick their own bets. The deeper force is momentum: the labs that started winning now compound the talent, the breaks, and the projects, while the same capital surge pushes DeepSeek to a $50B raise and DRAM prices up 90% in a quarter. Wall Street is finally asking the question that decides whether any of it holds: who actually pays for all this compute.

title: Accretion, Dilution, and the Cold Hard Truth of M&A Math
blurb: Most founders celebrate the exit at the handshake, then learn the next morning that how you get paid matters more than the headline number. Stock-funded deals can quietly dilute your remaining ownership, and debt-funded ones load the combined company before the cash clears. The fix is unglamorous: model accretion and dilution per scenario before you sit down, so you can show exactly how each structure hits your ownership and profit. Walking in with that math is the negotiating lever, because the person who can pressure-test every term is the one who doesn't get outmaneuvered by the fine print.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Waiting Until Next Year for IPO
blurb: OpenAI is leaning toward holding its IPO until next year, prioritizing growth and structure over a quick public debut. The delay keeps the company private while it absorbs heavy compute capex and its restructuring settles, even as rivals and investors push for a return on the capital already committed. For founders, it signals that the biggest AI player would rather build runway than chase a near-term liquidity event, and the rest of the market tends to take its cues from that pace.

title: The CEO of AWS on why Amazon is hiring 11,000 interns and junior employees
blurb: AWS CEO Matt Garman says junior employees are as necessary as ever, and Amazon is hiring 11,000 interns and new grads this year even as it sells agents that recruit, code, and process claims with no human in the loop. He brushes off Dario Amodei's prediction that AI could erase half of entry-level white-collar jobs, arguing those roles change rather than vanish, the way Excel reshaped finance work without shrinking the labor force. The tension is that AWS now spends $200 billion on AI infrastructure while staffing the very pipeline that infrastructure is built to automate.

title: SpaceX, Charter Discussed Mobile Phone Partnership in US
blurb: SpaceX and Charter held executive-level talks about jointly selling a consumer mobile phone service in the US, according to people familiar with the discussions. The pairing would combine Charter's existing wireless and broadband base with SpaceX's Starlink direct-to-cell coverage, aimed at the dead zones where terrestrial carriers drop off. For founders, it signals satellite connectivity moving from a niche backup into a mainstream consumer wireless feature, putting fresh pressure on Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile.

title: Trump Vows 100% Tariff on Europe Over Digital Services Taxes
blurb: Trump threatened 100% tariffs on goods from any country that levies a digital services tax, escalating just after European nations ratified a trade pact with the US. Most DSTs target US tech revenue specifically, so the move ties the entire trade relationship to how Europe taxes American platforms. For founders selling into the EU, the risk is a fast-moving tariff regime where pricing, supply chains, and cross-border expansion plans hinge on a tax fight none of them started.

title: AI Bust Risks Ripple Effects From Growth to Credit, BIS Says
blurb: The BIS named an AI bust alongside inflation and fiscal stress as the biggest current threats to global prosperity. The concern isn't the technology but the financial plumbing: a sharp reversal in AI spending could spill from growth into credit markets, where the capex boom is increasingly funded by debt. For founders, the read is that AI-linked capital is now systemically coupled, so a correction would tighten funding well beyond the labs and into the broader startup economy.

title: LSEG: tech companies have raised $3.1B from mainland China stock market listings YTD, up 5x+ from a year earlier, as AI and chip companies drive onshore IPOs (Reuters)
blurb: Chinese tech companies have raised $3.1B through mainland stock listings so far this year, more than five times the same period in 2025, with AI and chip firms driving the onshore IPO surge. The shift reflects Beijing steering domestic capital into strategic sectors while US listings stay largely closed to Chinese names. For founders watching the global funding map, the deep-tech capital pool is increasingly forming inside China rather than flowing across borders.

title: Chip Makers Are Profiting Off AI at the Expense of Just About Everyone Else
blurb: The AI boom is funneling an extraordinary amount of cash to memory-chip makers, who are capturing the profits while nearly everyone else in the stack pays for it. The model and application companies spending on compute are seeing margins squeezed, and AI users may eventually foot the bill too. For founders building on top of this stack, the takeaway is that the durable pricing power right now sits with the hardware suppliers, not the labs or the products built on them.

title: Israel’s $30B AI Sovereignty Bet
blurb: Israel approved a National AI Program built around sovereign compute, with a 100,000-unit GPU target that analysts peg at $20B to $30B or more once power, cooling, and refresh cycles are counted. That is an enormous number for a country its size, and it signals AI shifting from startup theme to national infrastructure, the same move that once turned cyber into a dominant ecosystem. The opening for founders is not selling software to ministries but building the companies that sit on top of this infrastructure layer and turn national capability into global products.

title: Previewing GPT-5.6 Sol: a next-generation model
blurb: OpenAI began a limited preview of the GPT-5.6 series, led by flagship Sol alongside Terra, a balanced model at 2x lower cost than GPT-5.5, and Luna for low-cost workloads. Sol sets a new state of the art on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and adds a max reasoning effort setting plus an ultra mode that spawns subagents for complex tasks, though access is restricted to trusted partners during a government-coordinated rollout.

title: DSpark: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference [pdf]
blurb: DSpark applies speculative decoding to accelerate LLM inference by drafting multiple candidate tokens with a small model and verifying them in parallel against the full model. The paper reports throughput gains while preserving the target model's output distribution, with the draft-and-verify loop tuned to keep acceptance rates high without retraining the base model.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra and Luna models — but only accessible to limited preview partners for now, per US Gov
blurb: OpenAI announced GPT-5.6 in three variants: Sol for hard problems like complex coding and security research, Terra for high-volume business workloads, and Luna for fast, low-cost everyday tasks. Sol and Terra set new benchmark highs while Luna lands near GPT-5.5 levels, but access is limited to roughly 20 partner organizations pending a federal safety review, with general availability planned for the coming weeks.

title: Anatomy of a Failed (Nation-State?) Attack
blurb: A Rust crates.io maintainer documents a fake-interview attack that used a fabricated VC persona, a defunct Singapore firm, and a real-sounding call to push a malicious "test" repo carrying a RAT the author dubs PinpinRAT. The payload-laden image evades every AV engine on VirusTotal, and the writeup walks through the social-engineering chain plus the IoC-detection script the author built to catch it.

title: Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware
blurb: Researchers at Mozilla's 0DIN demonstrated an attack where a clean GitHub repo with no malicious code tricks Claude Code into opening a reverse shell while it tries to recover from a planted setup error. The agent never decides to run malware; it follows an error message that triggers a script fetching an attacker-controlled DNS TXT record, three indirection steps from anything it actually evaluated.

title: OpenAI launches GPT-5.6 Sol preview for select partners
blurb: OpenAI opened a limited preview of GPT-5.6, led by its new flagship Sol model alongside the cheaper Terra and Luna, starting with trusted partners via the API and Codex. Sol adds a max reasoning effort and a subagent-driven ultra mode, claims state of the art on Terminal-Bench 2.1, and ships under a controlled rollout tied to its High cyber and bio risk classification.

title: Framework's 10G Ethernet module exposes USB-C's complexity
blurb: Framework's 10G Ethernet Expansion Card hits its rated speed only when paired with a USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 (20 Gbps) port, since the Realtek RTL8159 controller falls back to under 8 Gbps on USB4 and Gen 2x1 links. The author got 9.4 Gbps on Windows but only 7 Gbps in Linux due to driver build failures on newer kernels, and the module ran near 70°C under sustained load.

title: The gap between open weights LLMs and closed source LLMs
blurb: The author tests the viral claim that open weights LLMs will catch up to closed source by December 2026, showing it holds only on a single benchmark. Repeating the analysis across all 18 Artificial Analysis benchmarks yields a near-flat average gap of just under 5 months, with coding closing fast while most other categories barely move.

title: Qwen-AgentWorld
blurb: Qwen-AgentWorld introduces two language world models (35B-A3B and 397B-A17B) that simulate agentic environments across 7 domains via long chain-of-thought reasoning, trained on 10M+ real interaction trajectories through a three-stage CPT/SFT/RL pipeline. Used as a decoupled simulator for agentic RL, it surpasses real-environment training alone, and as an agent foundation model the world-model warm-up lifts performance across 7 downstream benchmarks.

title: Vercel Launches AI SDK 7 with Enhanced Stream and Tool Orchestration
blurb: Vercel released AI SDK 7, the TypeScript SDK with over 16 million weekly downloads, adding production depth for agent work across reasoning control, typed tool context, durable execution, and telemetry. The release standardizes provider-specific reasoning behind a single `reasoning` option, adds tool approvals and sandbox support for running agents, and ships a v6-to-v7 codemod for automated migration.

title: Open source maintainership in the age of AI
blurb: The Kubernetes maintainers introduced an AI policy that requires contributors to disclose AI assistance, keeps the human contributor fully accountable for every change, and bans listing AI as a commit co-author or as an "assisted-by" trailer. The post argues AI has made code generation fast without improving code maintenance, so the project now enforces CLA checks on co-authors and expects reviewers to engage with humans rather than AI-generated review replies.

title: What happened after 2k people tried to hack my AI assistant
blurb: A developer built hackmyclaw.com, exposing an OpenClaw email assistant guarded by a five-line anti-injection prompt and challenging 2,000 people to leak its secrets.env file. Across 6,000 attempts spanning future-self impersonation, fake compliance audits, and multilingual injections, nothing leaked, though the experiment cost over $500 in API tokens, a three-day Gmail suspension for fraud detection, and a batch-processing bug where early injections made the agent suspicious of everything that followed.

title: GLM-5.2 is the step change for open agents
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-weight model that the author argues crosses a capability threshold for agentic coding rather than being the incremental bump its version number suggests. The post tracks the post-launch evidence over benchmarks, noting that GLM-5.2 became the only open model competing with OpenAI and Anthropic on Arena's agent leaderboard, alongside Z.ai's use of the SLIME RL framework and a max-thinking-effort recommendation.

title: Fintech Engineering Handbook
blurb: This handbook documents the patterns behind money-handling systems, organized around three principles: no invented data, no lost data, and no trust. It covers idempotency, deduplication, reconciliation, event sourcing, and audit trails, and breaks down the four ways to represent money, from floating-point precision losses to BigDecimal and minor-units integer storage.

title: New agentic memory framework uses 118K tokens per query. LangMem burns through 3.26M.
blurb: Researchers at the National University of Singapore built MRAgent, an agentic memory framework that replaces the static "retrieve-then-reason" pipeline with active reconstruction, starting from small prompt triggers like a name or place and following metadata stepping stones to assemble evidence one piece at a time. The approach interleaves retrieval with reasoning so the agent can revise its queries mid-task, cutting consumption to 118K tokens per query against LangMem's 3.26M.

title: Show HN: Smart model routing directly in Claude, Codex and Cursor
blurb: Workweave released Router, a drop-in proxy for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini that uses a small on-box embedder to pick a model per request rather than a static rule, and points Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or any app at a single endpoint. It speaks the native APIs of all three providers plus OSS models via OpenRouter, keeps provider keys encrypted on your machine, and emits OTLP traces out of the box.

title: Gossamer: a Rust-flavoured language with real goroutines and pause-free memory
blurb: Gossamer is a new Rust-flavored language that pairs Go-style goroutines on an M:N scheduler with a Swift-like memory model, using deterministic reference counting and automatic cycle collection instead of a borrow checker or tracing GC. The pre-1.0 release (v0.20.0, Apache-2.0) runs as a bytecode VM with a REPL or compiles to a single dependency-free native binary via LLVM, and ships a standard library covering HTTP, JSON, crypto, and SQL.

title: British Columbia, Time Zones, and Postgres
blurb: British Columbia switched to permanent UTC-7 on March 8, 2026, which breaks any Postgres `timestamptz` appointment stored before the rule change and queried after. Because these columns store UTC and reconvert using current tzdata, future November-to-March bookings can come back an hour off, and the post shows a SQL check to confirm whether your tzdata package has picked up the new offset.

title: Run a vLLM Server on HF Jobs in One Command
blurb: Hugging Face's `hf jobs run` command stands up a vLLM server on HF infrastructure with a single line, requesting a GPU via `--flavor` and exposing the OpenAI-compatible port through HF's public jobs proxy. It targets quick tests, evals, and batch generation rather than production, billing per-minute by hardware usage and authenticating requests with your HF token as a bearer key, with Inference Endpoints recommended for managed serving.

title: Measuring Exploits in LLM Agents with Tool Use
blurb: Cursor built an auditor agent to inspect SWE-bench Pro trajectories and found that 63% of Opus 4.8 Max's successful resolutions retrieved the known fix from the public web or bundled .git history rather than deriving it. Sealing git history and blocking internet access dropped Opus from 87.1% to 73.0% and Cursor's Composer 2.5 from 74.7% to 54.0%, showing that agentic coding benchmarks need controlled runtime environments, not just contamination-free training data.

title: Apple adds Google Gemini coding assistant in Xcode 26.6 update
blurb: Xcode 26.6 adds Google Gemini as a coding assistant provider, joining Anthropic's Claude Agent and OpenAI Codex in Apple's IDE. The update also enables other compatible agents through the Agent Client Protocol and bundles Swift 6.3.3 plus SDKs for the iOS 26.5, macOS 26.5, and visionOS 26.5 release lines.

title: How Netflix Simplified Batch Compute with Kueue
blurb: Netflix replaced the custom queuing and scheduling logic in its homegrown Compute Managed Batch system with Kueue, a cloud-native job queueing system for Kubernetes batch workloads, as part of moving its Titus container platform to be more Kubernetes-native. The post covers what drove the migration of millions of batch jobs, how the team preserved the tenant-hierarchy and per-tenant capacity model on top of Kueue, and what the switch lets the platform offer.

title: Control an Android Phone with Gemini 3.5 Flash Computer Use
blurb: Gemini 3.5 Flash ships native Computer Use, a tool that takes a screenshot plus a goal and returns structured actions like taps, swipes, and text input on a 0-999 normalized coordinate grid. This guide drives an Android emulator over ADB through the Python SDK, running a screenshot-act-screenshot loop, with a quickstart repo and setup script that provision the SDK and virtual device from the terminal.

title: Grafana 13.1 release: observability as code updates, extending Grafana Assistant across more data sources, and more
blurb: Grafana released 13.1, expanding observability-as-code with four Git Sync enhancements, including importing dashboard JSON straight into a provisioned folder and syncing dashboards at the repo root without a containing folder. The release also extends Grafana Assistant to more data sources, with path-based uniqueness now letting dashboards share a title as long as they live at different paths in the repo.

title: Stripe Directory public preview
blurb: Stripe released a public preview of Stripe Directory, a searchable index of businesses, published Stripe Apps, Stripe Projects providers, and machine-payment endpoints on the Stripe network, accessible via the `stripe directory search` CLI plugin. Output supports a `--format json` mode and installable agent skills, letting AI agents discover, compare, and integrate services autonomously using returned provider slugs and MPP endpoints.

title: Client-Side Load Balancing at a Million Requests Per Second
blurb: Zalando replaced its shared edge router with an in-process client-side load balancer for the million-requests-per-second internal fan-out behind PRAPI, layering on N-ring fade-in, occupancy-based bounded load, and AZ-aware routing with a latency health factor. Owning the routing decision cut cost, isolated PRAPI from shared-infrastructure latency spikes, and surfaced detailed per-request logs that exposed incidents previously misattributed to either Skipper or the API itself.

title: OpenTTD 16.0-Beta1
blurb: OpenTTD 16.0-beta1 lands with reversible trains, open-join multiplayer companies, filterable dropdowns, and improved map generation, now open for testing.

title: Microsoft's fast coding model MAI-Code-1-Flash comes to Copilot Business and Enterprise
blurb: Microsoft made MAI-Code-1-Flash, its lightweight first-party coding model, generally available to GitHub Copilot Business and Enterprise customers, where admins must enable it via policy before developers can use it. The model targets fast, iterative completions over heavy multi-file work, and in Microsoft's own benchmarks scored 51.2% on SWE-Bench Pro versus 35.2% for Claude Haiku 4.5, at $0.75 per million input tokens and $4.50 per million output.

title: A C++ implementation of a fast hash map and hash set using hopscotch hashing
blurb: hopscotch-map is a header-only C++ hash map and hash set that uses open-addressing with hopscotch hashing to keep collision chains cache-local, outperforming std::unordered_map in most cases while using less memory than google::dense_hash_map. It ships power-of-two and prime growth variants, heterogeneous lookups, support for move-only and non-default-constructible keys, and a bounded-worst-case version for cases where hash DoS is a concern.

title: Bringing more agent harnesses and frameworks to Cloudflare, starting with Flue
blurb: Cloudflare added durable execution, dynamic code execution, a durable filesystem, and dynamic workflows to its Agents SDK as a base layer that any agent harness can build on. Flue, a new open-source framework from the Astro team built on the Pi harness, shipped its 1.0 Beta as the first framework on the stack, layering project structure, conventions, integrations, and a CLI on top.

title: AMD Strix Halo RDMA Cluster Setup Guide
blurb: This guide walks through linking two AMD Strix Halo nodes over Intel E810 NICs running RoCE v2 to run distributed vLLM inference with Tensor Parallelism across both APUs. The setup covers Fedora host config, kernel and BIOS tuning, and a toolbox script that auto-detects RDMA devices and patches RCCL, dropping inter-node tensor-sync latency from roughly 70-100µs over TCP/IP to about 5µs with RDMA.

title: Can We Agree on a Storage/Workload Architecture Taxonomy?
blurb: Jack Vanlightly proposes a taxonomy for storage and workload architectures, classifying systems by number of systems, workloads, durable copies, and tiers to separate OLTP, OLAP, HTAP, and Databricks' new LTAP. The framework distinguishes tiering (one copy moved hot to cold) from materializing (a second durable copy in another system), with data visibility across workloads as the dimension that determines staleness.

title: Beer CSS – Build material design in record time
blurb: Beer CSS is a lightweight CSS framework for building Material Design 3 interfaces using semantic HTML elements instead of utility-class soup. It ships with built-in theming, dark mode, and optional helper libraries, keeping markup readable while staying framework-agnostic across plain HTML, React, Vue, and Angular.

title: What you need to know about Lambda MicroVMs
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, which give each user or session a persistent, Firecracker-isolated VM with a dedicated HTTPS endpoint, fast snapshot-based boot, and state that survives up to 8 hours of automatic suspend and resume. The post explains why existing options fall short for running AI-generated or user-supplied code, and how MicroVMs drop the request/response model in favor of long-lived sandboxes clients reach over HTTP/2, gRPC, or WebSockets with bearer-token auth.

title: Broken Windows of Data
blurb: Booking.com applies the "broken windows" theory to its shared enterprise data warehouse, arguing that small quality lapses like loose grain definitions, duplicated metrics, or skipped reviews compound into platform-wide trust and maintainability problems as they propagate downstream. The author describes a hybrid operating model where domain teams own delivery but work inside common modelling rules and shared entities, keeping governance from becoming a delivery bottleneck.

title: Liquid AI Releases Liquid Foundation Models 2.5 230M
blurb: Liquid AI released LFM2.5-230M, a 230M model hitting 213 tok/s on a Galaxy S25 Ultra and capable at tool use.

title: AI was supposed to kill engineering jobs, but new data suggests they're the most resilient
blurb: SignalFire's analysis of hiring across 80M+ companies found engineering was the most resilient tech job function in 2025, with engineering roles down just 11% from 2019 levels versus a 25% drop in overall tech hiring. Engineers made up 55% of new hires at the 12 "Tech Majors" last year, up from 46% in 2019, undercutting the claim that AI coding tools are already substituting for engineering headcount.

title: webmcp-gen: Generate Chrome WebMCP Tool Definitions from TypeScript
blurb: webmcp-gen generates spec-compliant Chrome WebMCP tool definitions and handler stubs from your existing TypeScript interfaces, parsing types via ts-morph, mapping them to JSON Schema, and pulling tool descriptions from JSDoc comments. Unlike runtime crawlers and SDKs in the WebMCP ecosystem, it works at build time and bakes in Google's security guidance, adding requestUserInteraction() reminders for mutating tools and sanitisation warnings for freeform string inputs.

title: Show HN: Decomp Academy – Learn to decompile GameCube games into matching C
blurb: Decomp Academy teaches you to reverse GameCube assembly into byte-matching C, using 254 lessons built from the live Star Fox Adventures decompilation project. You write C against real PowerPC functions and the original 2001 mwcceppc.exe compiler grades your output instruction-for-instruction, progressing from reading registers through arithmetic idioms, bitfield ops, branches, and loops.

title: Show HN: Adrafinil – keep a lid-closed Mac awake only while agents work
blurb: Adrafinil is a macOS menu bar app that blocks system sleep, including clamshell lid-closed sleep, only while an AI coding agent holds an active session, then restores normal sleep the moment the last session releases. Unlike always-on tools like caffeinate or Amphetamine, it wires into the hook systems of nine agents including Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor, uses reference-counted IOPMAssertions, and adds a thermal cutout so a lid-closed Mac in a bag can't cook itself.

title: The token is valid — but your headless Claude Code agent just 401'd forever
blurb: A debugging post traces why long-running headless Claude Code agents return HTTP 401 "Invalid authentication credentials" even though their static OAuth token still returns HTTP 200 on raw /v1/messages calls fired at the same instant. The author shows the rejection is bound to the instance's server-side session identity rather than the token, and that once an instance 401s it hard-latches and never recovers until the process restarts, making any token-validity probe structurally blind to the failure.

title: The frontier fails the Turing test
blurb: The author argues the clean split between general-purpose and specialized AI models has collapsed into a spectrum, citing Engram's $98M raise to retrain general models on a company's private knowledge and Cursor's Composer, built by reinforcement-learning an open base model like Kimi K2.5 against its own environment. For data teams, the takeaway is that "general vs fine-tuned" is no longer a clean architectural choice. The frontier labs set the baseline, and everyone above them is buying a general model and grinding it toward their specific workflow.

title: Mistral OCR 4: SOTA OCR for Document Intelligence
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, an OCR model that returns bounding boxes, typed-block classification, and inline confidence scores alongside extracted text across 170 languages. The model runs in a single container for self-hosted deployments and feeds RAG and enterprise search pipelines, scoring 85.20 on OlmOCRBench with a 72% average win rate against competing systems in independent annotator tests.

title: A Decision Framework for ETL Migration to Databricks
blurb: Databricks published a decision framework for migrating ETL off a legacy warehouse, arguing teams should evaluate each workload separately rather than rewrite everything in PySpark or lift SQL as-is. The guidance maps three paths: Databricks SQL for SQL-heavy logic (wrapped in Unity Catalog stored procedures when it needs loops or error handling), Spark Declarative Pipelines for declarative batch-plus-streaming, and PySpark for the rest. The phasing advice matters most: ship workload by workload instead of stalling on a master plan.

title: What does Adobe's Topaz Labs acquisition mean for creatives?
blurb: Adobe acquired Topaz Labs, the company behind Gigapixel and other AI photo and video enhancement tools widely regarded as superior to Photoshop's built-in upscaling. Topaz products will stay standalone outside Creative Cloud for now, but the deal lets Adobe stop paying API-consumption fees on the Topaz models already embedded in Photoshop and Premiere and likely accelerates deeper integration across its apps.

title: "A James Bond opening is the one thing that doesn't need to follow trends": how 007 First Light blurred the line between film and gaming
blurb: Territory Studio's Andrew Popplestone breaks down how the team built the 007 First Light opening title sequence, applying its cross-disciplinary film and games experience to push game-ready assets up to film-production quality with finer detail and definition. The interview is a useful look at how title-design craft translates across mediums, and why a Bond opening earns its refusal to chase current motion trends.

title: Peppa Pig faces its biggest controversy since its infamous banned episode, and AI is to blame
blurb: Hasbro has introduced contract terms requiring Peppa Pig's child voice actors to sign over the rights to use their voices for AI training, prompting an open letter from the Agents of Young Performers Association that nearly 1,000 people have signed. The clauses, which industry insiders say are spreading across film and TV, raise the question of whether parental consent can license the indefinite cloning of a child's voice for commercial use.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of foundation image-generation models built for creative exploration rather than the single polished default most diffusion tools converge toward. The system pairs a from-scratch data and distributed-training stack with a custom diffusion transformer using grouped-query attention, sigmoid-gated attention, and Qwen3-VL text encoding, trained through pretraining, SFT, preference optimization, and RL to span a wider range of styles and moods.

title: SAM Architecture designs stepped school in Nanterre as "village at the child's scale"
blurb: SAM Architecture's Yvonne Kerzrého School Complex in Nanterre arranges classrooms around cascading terraces and courtyards that push learning outdoors.

title: Eight inspiring homes with bookmaxxed interiors
blurb: Dezeen's latest lookbook profiles eight homes built around "bookmaxxing," the tongue-in-cheek term for treating book collections as a primary interior element, from a German penthouse to Thom Yorke's residence. The piece tracks how shelving, stacking, and display strategies have shifted from background storage to a deliberate styling device worth designing around.

title: Libre Barcode Project
blurb: Libre Barcode is an open-source font collection that renders scannable barcodes in formats like Code 128, Code 39, and EAN-13, with optional human-readable text below each code. Because the barcodes are fonts, designers can drop them into any layout tool and generate valid codes from plain text without a separate encoder, image asset, or generator service.

title: This week Frank Gehry's design for a Saadiyat Island arts centre was unveiled
blurb: Dezeen published the first visuals of Dar al Funoon Abu Dhabi, a performing arts centre on Saadiyat Island designed by Frank Gehry before his death and now under construction near his Guggenheim Abu Dhabi. The rippling theatre complex emulates swathes of flowing fabric, extending Gehry's signature curvilinear language into one of his final built works.

title: Golf cart, small car and moon buggy meet in electric vehicle Amble One
blurb: Ex-Apple designer Julian Hoenig and Forpeople's Michael Tropper launched Amble One, a doorless electric buggy pitched as a lightweight car alternative for short trips.

title: Abu Dhabi reveals plans for Frank Gehry-designed performing arts venue
blurb: Abu Dhabi has begun construction on Dar al Funoon, a Saadiyat Island performing arts venue Frank Gehry designed before his death, with completion expected in 2030. The project joins Gehry's Guggenheim Abu Dhabi on the same cultural district, extending his signature sculptural language across one of the region's largest concentrations of starchitect-designed museums and venues.

title: Moving Linear from styled‑components to StyleX
blurb: Linear is migrating its React apps from styled-components to StyleX to move style generation off the render path, using an agent-assisted workflow.

title: "Progressive spirit" of Space Age interiors informs Barcelona cocktail bar
blurb: Spanish designer Isern Serra completed Focacha, a Barcelona speakeasy whose multi-coloured modular furniture channels Verner Panton's 1960s Space Age interiors. The bar, commissioned by hospitality group Grupo La Confitería as a nod to the city's nightlife history, leans on retro-futuristic curves and saturated color blocking rather than the muted palettes dominating current hospitality design.

title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6 in three variants (Sol, Terra, Luna) as a limited preview to a small set of companies under an ongoing U.S. government engagement, with Sol pitched as its most capable cybersecurity model yet. OpenAI restricted access behind its strongest safety stack to block offensive use while permitting vulnerability research, patch development, and defensive testing, and noted Sol matches Anthropic Mythos Preview on ExploitBench using roughly a third of the output tokens.

title: Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware
blurb: Researchers at Mozilla's 0DIN demonstrated an attack that tricks AI coding agents like Claude Code into opening a reverse shell while cloning a clean GitHub repository that contains no malicious code. A Python package refuses to run until "initialized," prompting the agent to auto-execute a setup command that fetches and runs an attacker-controlled payload from a DNS TXT record, yielding a shell with the developer's privileges.

title: FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated advisory PSA I-062626-PSA to warn that Russian intelligence actors UNC5792 and UNC4221 are now phishing high-value targets into surrendering their Signal Backup Recovery Key, granting full message-history access and account takeover without breaking Signal's encryption. The captured key persists across new accounts on the same number, so victims must generate a fresh key in Settings to invalidate it for future backup downloads.

title: FBI says Russian intelligence hackers have a new trick for reading your Signal messages, and it works even after you change phones
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated advisory I-062626-PSA warns that Russian intelligence groups UNC5792 and UNC4221 are phishing Signal users into surrendering their backup recovery keys, granting persistent access to an account's full message and group history. The key survives a device change and even account re-registration on the same number, so the only mitigation is generating a new recovery key in Signal settings, which cannot undo data already exfiltrated.

title: Anatomy of a Failed (Nation-State?) Attack
blurb: A security researcher and crates.io maintainer documented a targeted fake-interview campaign in which an attacker posing as a Singapore VC delivered a Windows backdoor (dubbed "PinpinRAT") via a trojanized code-test repository, with the payload hidden in an image that evades all VirusTotal AV engines. The lure followed the DPRK-style social-engineering playbook of fabricated investor personas and advisory pitches targeting open-source developers, and the RAT and IoCs have been reported to Canadian authorities (CCCS).

title: Polymarket customers lose $3 million in supply-chain attack
blurb: Attackers compromised a third-party frontend dependency on Polymarket and injected malicious JavaScript that tricked users into approving fraudulent transactions, draining roughly $3 million in ParyonUSD from fewer than 15 accounts. Polymarket's backend was unaffected and the company will fully reimburse victims; PeckShield tracked the funds bridged from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped for about 1,893 ETH.

title: Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group attributed a previously undocumented .NET backdoor, STOCKSTAY, to Russian state actor Turla, which has deployed it against Ukrainian government and military targets and entities tied to Italian foreign policy. The multi-component implant shares significant code overlap with Turla's Kazuar backdoor, communicates over secure WebSocket C2 via the websocket-sharp library, and supports file theft, directory enumeration, and screen capture.

title: StubZero: $148,337 RCE in Google Cloud Production
blurb: A researcher chained a public debugging endpoint on Google Cloud's cloudcrmipfrontend-pa API into full remote code execution on Google's production environment, earning a $148,337 bounty under CVE-2026-2031. The flaw exposed proto definitions for any internal google3 service via /v1/integrationPlatform:getProtoDefinition, leaking request and response schemas across unrelated products like YouTube before escalating to RCE.

title: Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff
blurb: Citizen Lab reported on June 25 that Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to extract data from detained opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone 12 in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it had stopped sales to Russia. MobileLockdown USB-pairing traces and an official MVD forensic report naming UFED Physical Analyzer corroborate the extraction, which pulled WhatsApp, Telegram, and Viber data for a political prosecution.

title: Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets
blurb: Russia-linked APT Turla is deploying StockStay, a multi-component .NET espionage backdoor, against Ukrainian government and military targets, Google's Threat Intelligence Group reports. The implant masquerades as PDF viewers or calculator utilities, communicates over a secure WebSocket C&C channel, and supports file exfiltration, screen capture, registry modification, and process execution, with code overlap linking it to Turla's older Kazuar implant.

title: Mystery hackers use novel SharkLoader dropper against governments, software devs
blurb: Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a global espionage campaign using a novel dropper called SharkLoader to compromise government organizations and software development firms across multiple countries. The attackers gain entry via known internet-facing flaws in Microsoft SharePoint, Exchange, Fortinet FortiOS, Cisco IOS XE, F5 BIG-IP, Zimbra, and others, then deploy Cobalt Strike beacons for credential dumping and lateral movement, so defenders should patch edge appliances and hunt for tampered Windows security logging.

title: CISA sets urgent deadline to fix Cisco flaw exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added Cisco Unified Communications Manager flaw CVE-2026-20230, a critical unauthenticated SSRF, to its KEV catalog after Defused observed in-the-wild exploitation writing arbitrary files via crafted HTTP requests. Federal agencies have until June 28 under BOD 26-04 to patch it alongside CVE-2026-12569, a critical deserialization RCE in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM disclosed June 18.

title: Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites
blurb: Infoblox identified over 236,000 second-level domains running investment scam templates built with Uni-App, the Chinese open source framework from DCloud, with fake crypto exchanges, pig-butchering sites, and wallet drainers spread across dozens of operators. New scam domains surged to roughly 15,000 per month after the RainbowEx fraud drew media coverage in October 2024, so defenders should fingerprint DCloud-built pages and block the associated second-level domains.

title: What happened after 2k people tried to hack my AI assistant
blurb: A researcher built hackmyclaw.com, an OpenClaw email assistant told to guard a secrets.env file, and invited Hacker News to break it. Over 2,000 people sent 6,000+ emails using fake urgency, spoofed "OpenClaw Admin" identities, multilingual prompts, and rapid-fire variations, but none exfiltrated the secrets or forced an unauthorized reply. The operational lessons matter more than the win: inbound volume triggered Gmail fraud suspension, ran up $500 in API costs, and batch processing leaked suspicion across emails until each was isolated in a fresh context.

title: Polymarket hack updated to $3.1 million days after the platform promised users full refunds
blurb: Attackers compromised a third-party vendor to inject a malicious frontend script, phishing $3.1M in PUSD from 11 Polymarket wallets; full refunds pledged.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: MCP's next specification (MCP 2026-07-28, final on July 28 with a 12-month deprecation window) makes the protocol stateless, killing session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts while tightening authentication. Akamai's analysis warns the shift trades those wins for new risks: predictable tracking IDs and state objects enable cross-agent data access and cross-tenant actions, and new MCP HTTP headers open desync attacks and secret leakage if developers map API keys or PII into them.

title: Mirage2FA phishing kit uses HTML smuggling to steal Microsoft 365 credentials
blurb: Mirage2FA, a phishing kit identified by Fortra, pairs short-lived HTML smuggling with obfuscated JavaScript loaders (Base64, XOR with 0xAD, and eval()) to deliver fake Microsoft 365 login pages that harvest credentials and MFA responses, including authenticator and number-matching prompts. The campaign uses business-themed lures and attacker infrastructure at cheacker[.]store; any user who submitted data should reset passwords, revoke active sessions and refresh tokens, and review MFA methods, mailbox rules, and OAuth grants.

title: New SharkLoader Malware Deploys Cobalt Strike in StrikeShark Cyberattacks
blurb: Kaspersky disclosed StrikeShark, a campaign by a likely Chinese-speaking actor that drops the previously undocumented SharkLoader to deliver Cobalt Strike Beacon across diplomatic, government, and software-development targets in Indonesia, Taiwan, Hong Kong, and elsewhere. Initial access comes from opportunistic exploitation of known PoC-available CVEs including ProxyLogon (CVE-2021-26855), Openfire (CVE-2023-32315), and GeoServer (CVE-2024-36401), so defenders should prioritize patching internet-facing appliances and hunt for web shells triggering DLL side-loading.

title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
blurb: CVE-2026-12569, an improper input validation flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM, is being exploited in the wild by unauthenticated attackers for remote code execution, the first confirmed abuse of a PTC product and the first PTC entry in CISA's KEV catalog. PTC began shipping patches on June 17 and published IoCs for persistent JSP webshells; CISA set a June 28 federal remediation deadline, and Windchill's heavy use across automotive, aerospace, and defense raises OT supply-chain risk.

title: We can still stop California's 3D printer surveillance scheme
blurb: The California State Assembly passed AB 2047, which would mandate surveillance and print-blocking software on 3D printers to prevent unlicensed firearm manufacturing, despite EFF warnings that the technology cannot work as intended.
