The bill now heads to the state senate after amendments that dropped the requirement for the software to actually prevent evasion, while leaving privacy, open source, and lawful-speech concerns unaddressed.

title: Polymarket Confirms $3 Million Loss From Third-Party Front-End Supply-Chain Breach
blurb: Polymarket confirmed that attackers stole roughly $3M in pUSD from fewer than 15 users by compromising a third-party frontend dependency that injected a malicious script, triggering wallet-approval prompts that routed funds to attacker wallets before bridging to Ethereum. The protocol's smart contracts and backend were untouched; Polymarket removed the affected dependency and pledged full refunds, marking its second security incident in two months.

title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
blurb: Microsoft warned of an active phishing campaign hitting hotels across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files containing disguised .lnk shortcuts to drop the Node.js-based TonRAT implant. The operators launder authentication by routing lures through Calendly and Google redirects to pass SPF/DKIM/DMARC, and TonRAT resolves C2 domains via the TON blockchain API to evade static blocklists.

title: Aggressive federal PQE timeline prompts warnings for enterprises
blurb: The Department of War announced a centralized oversight structure for post-quantum encryption and will fold PQC requirements into CMMC, building on an executive order mandating federal contractors meet NIST PQC standards by December 2030 for key establishment and 2031 for signatures. Gartner advises enterprises to build a PQC inventory and remediation program in 2026, move to automated cryptographic bills of materials by 2027, and brace for conflicting sovereignty-driven regulations as other governments follow.

title: Towards Native Post-Quantum Private ETH
blurb: An Ethereum researcher lays out the cryptographic work needed to enshrine post-quantum private transfers at L1, arguing turnstiles and HNDL-only defenses fall short.

title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
blurb: A supply-chain compromise of a third-party Polymarket vendor injected a malicious script into the prediction market's frontend for some users, enabling a phishing campaign that stole roughly $3M in pUSD from at least 11 victims. PeckShield reports the attacker bridged the funds from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped them into ~1,893 ETH; Polymarket says it removed the affected dependency and will fully refund those affected.

title: Even the Secret Service won't use company-issued phones
blurb: The US Secret Service has barred staff from using government-issued phones, with agents instead relying on personal devices for sensitive work. The policy underscores persistent distrust of managed mobile fleets even inside federal agencies, where device security and supply-chain concerns continue to shape operational practice.

title: ATF cancels controversial commercial geolocation contract
blurb: The ATF canceled its contract for Penlink's Webloc commercial location-surveillance tool, which used ad-tech data to track Americans' geolocation without a warrant. The cancellation followed congressional scrutiny from Sen. Ron Wyden and Rep. Michael Cloud, and came after the agency had already run more than 340 searches using the system, including 222 tied to active case numbers.

title: Anthropic gets US government's permission to redeploy its Mythos cybersecurity AI model
blurb: The US government authorized Anthropic to redeploy Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to more than 100 US organizations that operate and defend critical infrastructure, lifting the June 12 suspension that had cut off all access. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick approved the restart after daily talks over safeguards, following concerns the model could be jailbroken and that a China-linked group had gained access; Fable 5 access remains under negotiation with no timeline.

title: ZeroTier Quantum RC2 brings post-quantum security closer to general availability
blurb: ZeroTier released RC2 of ZeroTier Quantum, its end-to-end post-quantum networking platform, pairing ML-KEM-1024 with hybrid AES-384 and mutual identity authentication in a Rust, memory-safe architecture to counter "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks. The release meets NIST and NSA CNSA 2.0 standards ahead of 2027 federal PQC mandates, and a third-party code audit and penetration test remain before general availability.

title: Cisco Adds NHI to Security Stack With Astrix, WideField Acquisitions
blurb: Cisco agreed to acquire NHI-governance startup Astrix Security and identity-intelligence firm WideField, building out a control plane for non-human identities like API keys, service accounts, and OAuth apps tied to AI agents. WideField's session and activity telemetry will feed Splunk's agentic SOC, normalizing and correlating identity data so teams can monitor how machine and agent accounts behave over time.

title: A Mechanistic Explanation of Prompt Injection (and why you should study roles)
blurb: A research writeup argues that prompt injection is fundamentally a problem of how LLMs perceive roles, since models receive system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and their own reasoning as one undifferentiated token stream partitioned only by chat-template tags like <system> and <user>. The authors use this theory to construct new attacks, explain anomalous mech-interp results, and predict when injections succeed, and call for a research subfield focused on the "science of roles." Useful framing for defenders weighing why role-based input separation remains brittle.

title: Chinese cybersecurity company claims it’s built a better-than-Mythos bug finder
blurb: A Chinese cybersecurity firm claims it has built an AI-driven vulnerability finder that outperforms Anthropic's Mythos at discovering exploitable bugs in real codebases. The claim is unverified and lacks published benchmarks, but it signals that automated bug discovery is becoming a competitive front between US and Chinese labs, raising the prospect of faster offensive tooling on both sides.

title: New Initiative Tackles Security for End-of-Life Open Source Software
blurb: The Commonhaus Foundation launched the Open Source Sustainability Initiative, a collaborative program to help enterprises manage and secure open-source projects that have reached end-of-life but remain in production. The effort targets a growing gap: EOL components keep accruing CVEs after maintainers move on, a problem worsened by NIST's scaled-back CVE handling and open-source vulnerabilities per codebase more than doubling year-over-year.

title: Your First GRC Agent: A Red Teamer's Walkthrough
blurb: A red teamer argues that agentic AI suits GRC because the work is high-volume, repeatable, and run against known baselines, making it ideal for autonomy, real-time context, and multi-step execution rather than the point-in-time, scheduled artifacts legacy programs produce. The author stresses that judgment stays deterministic and human-owned: the model handles reasoning, summarization, and orchestration while controls, thresholds, and policy decisions come from analysts. The walkthrough builds a single agent that traces control drift and identifies evidence gaps.

title: In Other News: Chinese Mythos-Like AI, Tata Electronics Breach, Snyk Layoffs
blurb: SecurityWeek's weekly roundup covers a Chinese Mythos-style AI, a 630 GB Tata Electronics breach exposing Apple and Tesla data, and Snyk layoffs.

title: China Has Matched Anthropic in Cybersecurity, Resetting AI Race
blurb: Chinese AI labs have reportedly matched Anthropic's Mythos in cybersecurity capability, narrowing a gap that U.S. policymakers had assumed favored Washington. Critics argue that restrictions on top domestic AI models are slowing American development while Beijing faces no comparable constraints, potentially ceding an offensive cyberwarfare edge.

title: Researchers say Z.ai's GLM-5.2 matches latest US models at finding security bugs, as critics question the US' lax approach in restricting Chinese open models (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Researchers report that Z.ai's GLM-5.2 matches the latest US frontier models at finding security vulnerabilities, per a Wall Street Journal analysis. The result is fueling criticism that US export controls leave Chinese open-weight models largely unrestricted even as their offensive-security capabilities approach parity.

title: Choosing a Public DNS Resolver
blurb: A new guide ranks 29 public DNS resolvers against the criteria operators actually weigh: encrypted transport, DNSSEC validation, IPv6, jurisdiction, and operator type. Peer-reviewed measurements anchor the trade-offs: DoH and DoT add minor overhead while cutting in-transit tampering, but roughly 25% of DoT providers served invalid TLS certs, so favor validating, no-log operators and treat DNSSEC as mandatory.

title: CISA sets urgent deadline to fix Cisco flaw exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added two actively exploited or critical flaws to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, ordering federal agencies to patch a critical SSRF bug in Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CVE-2026-20230) and an RCE flaw in PTC's Windchill and FlexPLM systems (CVE-2026-12569) by June 28. The Cisco bug is unauthenticated and remotely exploitable, with attackers seen writing arbitrary files to affected endpoints since last weekend.

title: Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group attributed a new .NET backdoor, STOCKSTAY, to Russia's Turla group targeting Ukrainian government and military organizations.

title: FBI says Russian intelligence hackers have a new trick for reading your Signal messages, and it works even after you change phones
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated an advisory warning that Russian intelligence groups UNC5792 and UNC4221 are phishing Signal users for their backup recovery keys, which grants persistent access to an account's entire message history. The key survives a phone change and can only be neutralized by generating a new one in Signal settings, leaving IT security teams to push recovery-key hygiene for high-value staff and government-adjacent users.

title: Mystery hackers use novel SharkLoader dropper against governments, software devs
blurb: Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a global campaign using a previously unknown dropper called SharkLoader to compromise government organizations and software developers across multiple countries. The attackers exploit known, unpatched flaws in Microsoft SharePoint and Exchange, Fortinet FortiOS, Cisco IOS XE, and F5 BIG-IP, then deploy Cobalt Strike to steal credentials, underscoring the patch-management exposure in IT teams' internet-facing vendor stacks.

title: IBM details major chip breakthrough with new sub-1nm ‘nanostack’ 3D architecture
blurb: IBM detailed a sub-1nm "nanostack" 3D transistor architecture at the 0.7nm node, packing 100 billion transistors at nearly twice the density of its 2021 2nm chips and delivering up to 50% more performance or 70% better energy efficiency. The design vertically stacks transistors using different materials per layer and was experimentally validated in CMOS, positioning IBM's Albany research line for production as it installs ASML's High NA EUV lithography.

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
blurb: AWS introduced Lambda MicroVMs, a serverless primitive that gives each user or session a Firecracker-isolated, stateful execution environment with near-instant launch and resume. The service targets teams building AI coding assistants, sandboxed code environments, and data analytics platforms, removing the choice between VM-grade isolation and container launch speed without standing up custom virtualization infrastructure.

title: Soaring iPad, Xbox Prices Reveal Pain of Memory Chip Mess
blurb: Apple and Microsoft raised prices on iPads, Macs, and Xbox consoles within five hours of each other Thursday, passing soaring memory-chip costs directly to customers. The hikes signal that the DRAM and NAND shortage driven by AI-infrastructure demand is now hitting consumer hardware budgets, a cost pressure enterprise IT buyers should expect across device refresh cycles and procurement contracts.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: The Model Context Protocol will transition to a new enterprise-ready version, MCP 2026-07-28, on July 28, making the protocol stateless at the layer and giving SDK maintainers a 12-month window to migrate off legacy versions. Akamai's analysis finds the update closes several vulnerability classes, including session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts, but the new tracking identifiers and MCP-specific HTTP headers open fresh risks around predictable IDs, cross-tenant actions, and secrets leaking through misconfigured headers.

title: CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue
blurb: CISA added a critical PTC Windchill remote code execution flaw (CVE-2026-12569, CVSS 9.3) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, citing active exploitation that deploys JSP web shells via untrusted-data deserialization. It is the first PTC product to land in the KEV catalog, and although patches shipped last week, PTC confirmed continued attacks through June 25 and urged customers to block the C2 address 5.180.41.35 and scan for web shells immediately.

title: Securing agentic AI with perimeter guardrails: What's new in VPC Service Controls
blurb: Google Cloud added new VPC Service Controls capabilities for agentic AI, letting admins treat agents as first-class IAM identities in perimeter ingress and egress rules and enforce conditional access based on MCP attributes like tool name and read-only status. The update also natively integrates VPC-SC with the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, automatically blocking public internet access to protected instances and letting teams revoke a compromised agent's network access immediately.

title: Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, a shared open source Security Incident Response Team for coordinated discovery, patching, and disclosure of OSS vulnerabilities, backed by Anthropic, AWS, Google, Microsoft, IBM, Red Hat, Cisco, JPMorganChase, and others, with seed funding from its Alpha-Omega fund. The effort overlaps heavily with Chainguard's recently announced Athena coalition and reflects a shared goal of deploying fixes before AI-accelerated exploits weaponize public disclosures.

title: Mirage2FA phishing kit uses HTML smuggling to steal Microsoft 365 credentials
blurb: Fortra researchers identified Mirage2FA, a phishing kit that pairs short-lived HTML smuggling with obfuscated JavaScript loaders to serve fake Microsoft 365 login pages and harvest credentials during MFA prompts, including authenticator and number-matching flows. A successful capture enables full account takeover across email, Teams, SharePoint, and connected SaaS, so IT teams should reset exposed passwords, revoke active sessions and refresh tokens, and audit mailbox rules and OAuth grants.

title: U.S. partially reverses Anthropic AI ban for Mythos but keeps Fable 5 off the market
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department partially lifted its restriction on Anthropic's frontier models, clearing Claude Mythos 5 for redeployment to more than 100 vetted organizations that operate or defend critical infrastructure, while the public-facing Fable 5 stays off the market. The letter waives export-license requirements for approved companies' non-U.S. employees but keeps full availability and any timeline for broader access unresolved.

title: Trump Admin releases Anthropic  Mythos to be used by more than 100 US companies, agencies
blurb: The Trump administration partially reversed its export ban on Anthropic's cybersecurity models, clearing Claude Mythos 5 for use by more than 100 named US agencies and companies that operate critical infrastructure, including non-American employees at those organizations. The directive leaves Fable 5 still restricted and limits Mythos 5 to vetted partners rather than general release, so enterprises outside the approved list remain locked out of Anthropic's strongest security model.

title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-12569, an unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in PTC's Windchill and FlexPLM platforms, marking the first PTC product ever added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. PTC began shipping patches on June 17 and confirmed attackers are deploying persistent JSP webshells for command execution and data exfiltration, with CISA ordering federal agencies to remediate by June 28.

title: CL-STA-1062 Targets Southeast Asian Governments and Critical Infrastructure
blurb: Unit 42 attributed a 2025 campaign against Southeast Asian government entities and energy-sector state-owned enterprises to CL-STA-1062, a Chinese-speaking threat cluster active since 2022 that has deployed a previously undocumented backdoor called TinyRCT. The malware supports arbitrary command execution, file exfiltration, screen capture, and self-destruct, raising the threat profile for critical-infrastructure operators across the Asia-Pacific region.

title: Microsoft adds Copilot finance tools to Excel for M365 users
blurb: Microsoft made its Copilot finance tools for Excel generally available to Microsoft 365 Copilot customers on Web, Windows, and Mac, adding markdown-defined "skills" that automate workflows like DCF modeling and variance analysis, plus data connectors for FactSet, S&P Global, Morningstar, PitchBook, CB Insights, and Daloopa. The move pulls trusted financial data and repeatable modeling directly into Excel, encroaching on standalone FP&A and market-data tools that finance teams currently procure separately.

title: OpenClaw's Skill Marketplace and the Emerging AI Supply Chain Threat
blurb: Unit 42 found five malicious skills that evaded ClawHub's VirusTotal and ClawScan screening between February and May 2026, including two macOS infostealers with live C2 infrastructure and a scanner-evading skill padded past file-size thresholds. OpenClaw banned the accounts and is now partnering with NVIDIA to document and analyze every published skill, underscoring that agentic skill marketplaces are an emerging supply chain attack surface distinct from npm or PyPI.

title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
blurb: Microsoft warned of an active phishing campaign hitting hotels across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files to drop a Node.js implant called TonRAT onto front-desk machines. The operators route lures through Calendly and Google redirect services to pass SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, a technique Microsoft calls authentication laundering that lets messages clear standard email defenses.

title: Apple is reportedly looking to buy chips from a US-blacklisted Chinese company
blurb: Apple is reportedly seeking Trump administration clearance to buy memory chips from CXMT, a Chinese supplier on the Pentagon's 1260H list of companies tied to the People's Liberation Army. The move would diversify Apple beyond Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix amid a memory shortage that already forced hardware price hikes, but Congress is expected to object on national-security grounds.

title: “MS 마요라나 양자 칩 성과, 섣부른 결론”…학계서 비판 제기
blurb: A peer-reviewed Nature paper from St Andrews physicist Henry Legg argues that Microsoft's Topological Gap Protocol is flawed and that the data behind its Majorana quantum-chip claims could stem from other physical phenomena, casting fresh doubt on the path to a scalable quantum computer by 2029. Microsoft is standing by its roadmap and DARPA-backed research, but the critique reinforces long-running skepticism that enterprise-ready Majorana hardware remains far from procurement reality.

title: Nearly Half of LG Smart TV Apps Are Laced with Proxies
blurb: Security researchers at Spur found that nearly half of the apps on LG's smart TV platform bundle SDKs that turn the devices into residential proxy exit nodes, routing third-party traffic through users' home networks without clear disclosure. For enterprise IT, any LG TVs deployed in conference rooms or lobbies become potential conduits for unattributed traffic, expanding the network attack surface and complicating endpoint monitoring on corporate WiFi.

title: Aggressive federal PQE timeline prompts warnings for enterprises
blurb: The Department of War announced a centralized oversight structure for post-quantum encryption and will fold PQC requirements into CMMC, which federal contractors must have third-party certified starting this November. A presidential executive order now mandates NIST PQC compliance by 2030, and Gartner advises enterprises to build a PQC inventory and remediation program in 2026 and press vendors on their migration timelines.

title: Google Workspace Weekly Recap - June 26, 2026
blurb: Google's latest Workspace recap promotes Apps Script to a core service, bringing it under Cloud Terms of Service with enterprise data protection, admin controls, and standard technical support. Admins also gain incremental data exports to their own GCS buckets instead of full re-exports, a broader admin password reset alert covering all administrator roles, and a one-click Gemini formula-fix capability in Sheets.

title: FFmpeg Fixes PixelSmash Flaw in Widely Used Video Decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a high-severity heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder that JFrog used to achieve full remote code execution against a Jellyfin media server through its normal library-scan pipeline. Any application built on libavcodec is exposed, including Kodi, OBS Studio, Nextcloud, PhotoPrism, and OS thumbnail generators, so IT teams should prioritize updating FFmpeg across self-hosted media and collaboration stacks.

title: Oracle stock has worst week since 2001 dot-com bust as AI financing concerns escalate
blurb: Oracle posted its worst week on Wall Street since 2001, falling 19% as investors weigh roughly $130B in debt and negative free cash flow of nearly $24B against its AI infrastructure commitments to OpenAI. The selloff reflects doubt that record borrowing and 162% higher capex, at almost $56B this fiscal year, will convert into durable margins on lower-margin cloud offerings.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Four vulnerabilities in open-source LLMOps platform Dify let attackers siphon other tenants' data; patched in version 1.14.2.

title: Proposed US law would make AI risk reporting a legal obligation
blurb: US lawmakers introduced the AI Incident Reporting Act, which would require developers of designated "covered models" to report major safety and security incidents to the Commerce Department within seven days, with a 48-hour congressional notification for imminent harms. Reportable events include models attempting to evade oversight, resist shutdown, or steal model weights, putting frontier AI labs like OpenAI and Anthropic under a federal disclosure mandate tied to capability thresholds.

title: Even the Secret Service won't use company-issued phones
blurb: The US Secret Service reportedly sidesteps its own agency-issued phones in favor of personal devices, undercutting the mobile device management policies that are supposed to keep government endpoints locked down. The episode is a reminder for IT leaders that MDM enrollment and acceptable-use rules collapse when even high-security users route around managed hardware, leaving endpoint visibility and compliance enforcement as the harder problem than provisioning the devices.

title: ZeroTier Quantum RC2 brings post-quantum security closer to general availability
blurb: ZeroTier released release candidate 2 of ZeroTier Quantum, its end-to-end post-quantum networking platform, moving it one step from general availability with ML-KEM-1024 and hybrid AES-384 cryptography that meets NIST and NSA CNSA 2.0 standards. The Rust-built mesh targets enterprises facing 2027 federal PQC mandates, claiming 30% lower CPU usage than comparable software-defined networking and defending against "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks ahead of a pending third-party audit.

title: IoT Product Cybersecurity Guidelines for the Federal Government
blurb: NIST released the initial public draft of Special Publication 800-213r1, updating its cybersecurity guidance for IoT products acquired and deployed by federal agencies. The revision tightens device-capability requirements that contractors and vendors must meet to sell into government, giving enterprise IT buyers a reference baseline for evaluating IoT procurement and supplier security commitments beyond the federal market.

title: Cisco Adds NHI to Security Stack With Astrix, WideField Acquisitions
blurb: Cisco agreed to acquire Astrix Security and WideField Security to bring non-human identity governance and session intelligence into its security stack, targeting the API keys, service accounts, and AI agents that sit outside conventional IAM and SOC tooling. WideField's telemetry will fold into Splunk's Agentic SOC, positioning Cisco against identity vendors racing to make agent identity the primary access-control layer.

title: Senior execs worry about AI system lock-in
blurb: An IBM Institute for Business Value study found that more than 70% of senior executives consider switching away from their primary AI provider challenging, signaling growing concern about vendor lock-in. The findings raise procurement-stage questions about model portability and contract terms as enterprises standardize on providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, Microsoft, and Google before exit costs are fully understood.

title: Analog chipmaker Onsemi buys Synaptics in $7B all-stock deal to push into physical AI
blurb: Onsemi acquired Synaptics in a $7B all-stock deal, paying 1.35 Onsemi shares per Synaptics share to expand from analog automotive and industrial silicon into edge and physical AI. The deal pairs Onsemi's power and sensing chips with Synaptics' touch, voice, and human-interface technology, positioning the combined company to supply silicon for robots, drones, and autonomous vehicles, though Onsemi's stock fell more than 8% on the news.

title: Microsoft extends extended updates for Windows 10 in the most muted way imaginable
blurb: Microsoft quietly extended its paid Extended Security Updates program for Windows 10, giving enterprises still running the OS past its October 2025 end-of-support date another window to keep receiving security patches. The low-key rollout signals Microsoft would rather push migration to Windows 11, but it hands IT teams stuck on legacy hardware or app-compatibility blockers more runway before they face an unpatched fleet.

title: IBM claims world's first sub-1 nanometer chip technology
blurb: IBM unveiled a "nanostack" architecture at what it calls the 7-angstrom node, packing nearly 100 billion transistors onto a fingernail-sized chip for AI data centers, roughly double its prior generation's density. The "sub-1 nanometer" label is a performance-equivalent marketing node rather than a physical dimension, but the claimed gains in compute and energy efficiency matter for enterprises weighing data-center power budgets against AI workload growth.

title: Retirement of Azure DevOps issuer in Workload identity federation service connections
blurb: Microsoft will retire the Azure DevOps issuer in workload identity federation service connections on July 1, 2027, deprecating it July 1, 2026 as it standardizes on the Microsoft Entra issuer across Azure services. The change affects only Azure public cloud service connections using single-tenant Entra apps or managed identities, so enterprise platform teams must migrate affected federated credentials before retirement to avoid broken secretless CI/CD pipelines.

title: Choosing a Public DNS Resolver
blurb: A practical guide walks through choosing among 29 public DNS resolvers by what you prioritize: privacy, malware blocking, jurisdiction, DNSSEC, IPv6, or speed, backed by peer-reviewed measurement studies. The operational takeaway for IT teams: encrypted transports (DoH, DoT) add minor latency but resist tampering, DNSSEC validation should be treated as mandatory, and operator jurisdiction governs what can be compelled or logged. The NSA has warned that external resolvers bypass internal DNS filtering and inspection.

title: ‘Botsitting’: The AI time-savings killer only governance can stop
blurb: A new study from the Work AI Institute finds that digital workers save 11 hours a week with AI but lose 6.4 of them to "botsitting": feeding tools missing context, checking outputs, debugging mistakes, and cleaning up confident-but-wrong answers. The result is that only 13% of workers report significantly better outcomes, so CIOs aren't capturing the AI ROI they budgeted for. The author argues the fix is governance and training, not more tools.

title: Cisco cuts 500 Bay Area jobs in aggressive pivot to AI
blurb: Cisco will cut 471 Bay Area jobs effective July 13, part of its 4,000-role global restructuring around AI.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft identified a self-propagating worm it calls Crypto Clipper that spreads via malicious .lnk files on USB drives, monitors clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases, and exfiltrates credentials and screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy. The portable Tor client and absence of IP-based command-and-control let the malware double as a lightweight backdoor, evading network defenses that key on exposed C2 endpoints and complicating detection for enterprise security teams.

title: Solidigm VP talks PCIe 6.0 SSDs, next-gen floating gate NAND, liquid cooled storage and more —  Avi Shetty, VP of AI, Solutions & Market Enablement discusses the future of enterprise storage tech
blurb: Solidigm's AI VP details PCIe 6.0 SSDs, in-house floating-gate NAND, and liquid-cooled drives for data center storage.

title: F5 expands further into AI security with SurePath AI acquisition
blurb: F5 acquired SurePath AI, a startup that detects shadow AI and agents running on corporate networks, folding it into the AI security platform F5 launched this week to discover, test, and apply guardrails to enterprise AI. The deal extends F5's run of AI-security buys after last fall's CalypsoAI purchase and targets buyers tired of stitching discovery, testing, and guardrails together from four to six separate tools.

title: Sources: Salesforce staff worry Anthropic's Claude Tag could cannibalize Slackbot and give the AI firm more leverage over the enterprise software industry (Laura Bratton/The Information)
blurb: Salesforce employees are concerned that Anthropic's Claude Tag could cannibalize Slack's built-in Slackbot and hand the AI vendor more leverage over the enterprise software market, according to The Information. The worry reflects a broader tension for incumbents like Salesforce, whose own products increasingly depend on the same model providers now building competing assistants directly into the workflows enterprises already pay for.

title: Microsoft extends free Windows 10 security updates for a second year — program now ends on October 12, 2027
blurb: Microsoft quietly extended its free consumer Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program by a year, pushing critical-patch coverage to October 2027 via an unannounced edit to its support page rather than a formal announcement. The reprieve covers the roughly 400 million personal PCs that fail Windows 11's TPM 2.0 and processor requirements, but it excludes Active Directory, Entra-joined, and MDM-managed corporate devices, so IT fleets still need a paid commercial ESU path.

title: White House Asks OpenAI to Slow Roll New Model Release
blurb: The White House asked OpenAI to limit the release of GPT-5.6 to a government-vetted set of partners, with agencies approving access customer by customer during a preview period before any broader rollout weeks later. The request follows Trump's executive order directing AI vendors to submit frontier models for federal testing, signaling that enterprise access to the most capable models may increasingly route through government review.

title: The 33-year-old ex-Snap exec Nadella is trusting to fix Copilot now oversees 11,000 people
blurb: Microsoft promoted Jacob Andreou, a 33-year-old former Snap executive, to EVP of Copilot after one year, putting him over 11,000 people and a plan to ship a super app by end of summer. The bet matters because only 4.5% of Microsoft's 450M 365 customers pay for Copilot, and Jefferies' Brent Thill said the general perception is that "it stinks.

title: Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Researchers tied a new wave of supply chain attacks to the Mini Shai-Hulud/Miasma/Hades malware family, which trojanized two dozen npm packages including LeoPlatform and RStreams, plus a Go module, after compromising a maintainer's npm token and pushing poisoned versions within a six-second window. The malware skips lifecycle hooks in favor of binding.gyp install-time execution, then drops a "Run Copilot" GitHub Actions workflow to steal CI/CD secrets and developer credentials.

title: Open source maintainership in the age of AI
blurb: The Kubernetes project published its approach to AI-assisted contributions, centered on a formal AI policy that demands transparency and human accountability. Contributors must disclose AI use in PRs, cannot list AI as a commit co-author, and the project now runs CLA checks on co-authors to flag agent-generated PRs that aren't merge-ready. The core argument is that AI made code generation fast while doing nothing for maintainability, so a human must still own and understand every change.

title: Critical open-source projects get a new security framework
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, an industry initiative establishing a shared Security Incident Response Team and Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure process for open-source projects that underpin finance, healthcare, energy, and AI infrastructure. Founding members including AWS, Anthropic, GitHub, Google, IBM, Microsoft, and NVIDIA aim to fund and coordinate fixes for small maintainer teams as AI shortens the window between flaw discovery and exploitation.

title: The HTTP QUERY Method
blurb: IETF published RFC 10008 defining the HTTP QUERY method, a safe, idempotent alternative to POST that can be retried without risking partial state changes.

title: How Cloudflare responded to the “Copy Fail” Linux vulnerability
blurb: Cloudflare detailed how its Linux kernel patching cadence neutralized "Copy Fail" (CVE-2026-31431), a local privilege escalation bug, before public disclosure. An automated weekly build off LTS branches plus a four-week Edge Reboot Release cycle meant the fix was already deployed across most of its 6.12 fleet, with no exposure or service disruption. The retro argues that staying close to upstream LTS is the cheapest insurance against zero-day scramble.

title: Securing agentic AI with perimeter guardrails: What's new in VPC Service Controls
blurb: Google Cloud added agentic-AI capabilities to VPC Service Controls, letting teams treat agents as first-class IAM principals in ingress and egress perimeter rules and write conditional access policies on MCP attributes like mcp.toolName and mcp.tool.isReadOnly. The update also natively integrates the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, automatically blocking public internet access to protected instances and shrinking the blast radius when an agent is compromised.

title: OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all developers, letting them register and manage their own OAuth clients for scoped, delegated access to the Cloudflare API instead of relying on long-lived API tokens. The release adds a clearer consent screen, dashboard-based revocation, and app-ownership visibility to blunt OAuth phishing, targeting SaaS integrations, internal developer platforms, and the growing class of agentic tools that need delegated access.

title: Client-Side Load Balancing at a Million Requests Per Second
blurb: Zalando's engineering team replaced their shared Skipper edge router with an in-process client-side load balancer for the million-requests-per-second internal fan-out behind PRAPI, their product read API serving 25 European markets. They layered on N-ring fade-in, occupancy-based bounded load, and AZ-aware routing with a latency health factor. Owning the routing decision cut cost, made the service resilient to the infrastructure beneath it, and finally exposed root causes that were neither Skipper nor PRAPI.

title: Cloudflare teams up with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge on a privacy-first anti-bot protocol
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are co-developing PACT (Private Access Control Tokens), a protocol that issues anonymous tokens to prove a visitor is human or an authorized agent, replacing CAPTCHAs and forced logins without tracking browsing history. The push lands as Cloudflare Radar shows bots now drive roughly 58% of global HTTP requests against 42% from people, and the group plans to submit PACT for formal standardization.

title: YouTube Removes Dislike Button From Shorts, Adds Speed Controls and Clear Screen Mode
blurb: YouTube is rolling out a set of Shorts player updates, replacing the dislike button with the more granular "Not Interested" and "Don't recommend this channel" controls, swapping the thumbs-up for a heart, and adding 2x playback speed plus a distraction-free Clear Screen mode. The shift matters for creators because Shorts now occupy most of YouTube's homepage, with long-form placement narrowing from two rows of five videos in 2024 to one row of two to three today.

title: Trustpilot just partnered with Shopify to put its reviews where AI search engines are already looking
blurb: Trustpilot partnered with Shopify to let merchants display and manage verified reviews natively inside their stores, with the integration going live June 29 as click-throughs from AI search engines jumped 1,490% in its last fiscal year. The deal positions Trustpilot's review data as a trust layer for AI assistants that increasingly cite it, already the fifth most-referenced domain on ChatGPT, when recommending products to shoppers.

title: We Booked 614 Meetings With One Inbound Agent. Your “Contact Us” Form Is Costing You Deals.
blurb: SaaStr replaced its "contact us" form with an inbound AI agent built on Qualified, and for one event it booked 614 meetings at roughly $85K average ticket size across about 2.25M sessions and 402K interactions, run by a three-person team. The case against the form is speed: human round-robin routing took two to three days, by which point most inbound leads had cooled or talked to competitors. The agent qualifies, routes, and runs campaigns and discounts on its own, collapsing that lag to near-instant.

title: TBM 427: The Bottleneck Strike Again!
blurb: The author argues that "engineering is no longer the bottleneck" is a seductive but broken frame, because product development is not a linear production line with one clogged pipe waiting to be cleared. The work spans sensing, deciding, learning, and adapting, and its constraints are distributed and co-evolving, not a single logjam that moves predictably. The practical takeaway for PMs: treat a visible bottleneck as a symptom to diagnose rather than the problem to solve, and be skeptical of slides claiming the constraint has simply shifted.

title: Building Effective Human-Agent Teams
blurb: Anthropic argues that working with AI is shifting from a single-player setup, where one person prompts one chat window, to a multiplayer model where agents join team channels like Slack with their own credentials, memory, and broad context. The piece details what that requires in practice: persistent memory tuned to shared goals, ongoing access to organizational information, and team norms like working in public so agents can build understanding. For PMs, the takeaway is that designing for agents now means designing the workflows and access rules around them, not just the prompt.

title: Config 2026: New Materials, New Tools, and a More Expressive Canvas
blurb: Figma's Config 2026 introduced code layers that turn any design frame into an interactive, editable code element with a single click or prompt, alongside Figma Motion, shaders, generative plugins, and Weave tools on the canvas. For product teams, the convergence of design and code in one collaborative file shortens the prototype-to-build handoff and pulls more of the spec-to-working-software loop into a tool PMs and designers already share.

title: Polymarket customers lose $3 million in supply-chain attack
blurb: Polymarket pledged to fully reimburse users who lost an estimated $3 million after attackers injected malicious JavaScript into its frontend through a compromised third-party vendor, tricking users into approving fraudulent transactions. PeckShield traced the theft to roughly $3 million in ParyonUSD bridged from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped into 1,893 ETH, with Bubblemaps counting fewer than 15 affected accounts.

title: Chainlink Launches Project Pangea With 50+ Banks Across 16 Countries for T+0 FX Settlement
blurb: Chainlink launched Project Pangea with a consortium of 50+ banks across 16 countries, targeting T+0 atomic settlement for the $9.6T-per-day global FX market via direct PvP swaps of regulated EUR and KRW stablecoins. The stack pairs Chainlink's CCIP, Data Streams, and Runtime Environment with Swift ISO 20022 messaging and AMM settlement contracts on Ethereum, Polygon, and a dedicated Pangea L1 operated by FairSquareLab.

title: Coinbase and OKX try to lure in Binance’s 450 million EU users after it failed to secure a MiCA license
blurb: Coinbase and OKX began offering sign-up bonuses to European users after Binance said it would suspend some services in the EU because it lacks a MiCA license by the July 1 deadline. Coinbase, MiCA-licensed since 2025, is pitching a 5% transfer bonus across several major markets and the UK, while OKX is matching deposits up to 8% for eligible EEA users.

title: Aave Confirms Aavenomics 3.0 Is Live With Buybacks and DAO Spending Cut
blurb: Aave activated Aavenomics 3.0, replacing its committee-directed buyback program with an immutable mechanism that automatically routes all protocol and GHO revenue to AAVE holders. Governance also cut the annual buyback budget from roughly $50M to $30M and shifted funding toward ETH-correlated assets, against protocol revenue running near $402M annualized.

title: Strategy's Enterprise mNAV Drops Below 1 for the First Time
blurb: Strategy's enterprise mNAV fell below 1 for the first time, with combined equity, convertible debt, and preferred stock totaling roughly $51.2B against a bitcoin treasury of about $51.3B at 847,363 BTC. The sub-1 ratio severs the equity-accretion channel the model depends on, since issuing new shares no longer buys more than a dollar of bitcoin per existing holder, with MSTR closing at an $82.31 52-week low.

title: Strategy loses its bitcoin premium as enterprise mNAV dips below 1
blurb: Strategy's modified net asset value slipped below 1, meaning its market cap now trades under the value of its bitcoin holdings and erasing the premium that long funded its debt-and-equity buying strategy. The STRC preferred shares briefly hit a record low of $71.40 on Friday, roughly 25% below par, signaling weakening investor appetite for the firm's leveraged BTC accumulation.

title: StablecoinX Begins Nasdaq Trading as First Public ENA Treasury Vehicle
blurb: StablecoinX began trading on Nasdaq under ticker USDE after closing its SPAC merger with TLGY Acquisition Corp., becoming the first public company structured around holding Ethena's ENA governance token. It holds roughly 3.03 billion ENA worth about $275M, around 20% of total supply, and gives equity investors regulated Ethena-ecosystem exposure without direct token custody.

title: Base Suffers Second Chain Halt in 24 Hours, Complicating B20 Activation Window
blurb: Base, the Coinbase-incubated Ethereum L2, halted block production twice in 24 hours on Thursday and Friday, with the second 14-minute stall showing "similar symptoms" to the first two-hour outage and indicating the root cause was not fully fixed. The repeat failures landed hours before the scheduled 18:00 UTC mainnet activation of B20, a native token standard for stablecoins and tokenized real-world assets, with no postmortem published.

title: Tether putting $23 billion gold stockpile to work with bullion-backed loans
blurb: Tether is bringing its tokenized gold product XAUT to crypto lender Ledn, letting holders borrow against their bullion without selling, with loans expected to launch later this year. The move monetizes Tether's $23B physical gold reserve, roughly 140 metric tons, and extends the firm's expansion beyond USDT into a broader finance, energy, and AI infrastructure group.

title: Polymarket hack updated to $3.1 million days after the platform promised users full refunds
blurb: Hackers stole roughly $3.1 million in Polymarket's PUSD token from 11 user wallets after a compromised third-party vendor injected a malicious script into the platform's frontend, with the funds bridged from Polygon to Ethereum. Polymarket pledged full refunds to affected PUSD holders and removed the dependency, though the breach lands amid a reported federal investigation into the prediction market's allegedly deceptive social media promotions.

title: Strategy's valuation has fallen below the value of its bitcoin holdings
blurb: Strategy's enterprise mNAV fell below 1 for the first time, with enterprise value at roughly $50.4B against bitcoin holdings worth about $51.1B at $60,000 per coin. MSTR trades near $82, down about 85% from its November 2024 high, making further share issuance dilutive to common stockholders and raising concerns the firm now trades like a discounted closed-end fund.

title: Canton Network Tops Blockchain Fee Rankings With $60M in 30 Days
blurb: Canton Network generated $60.2M in fees over the trailing 30 days, surpassing Tron's $27.6M and Ethereum's $11.3M to top DefiLlama's leaderboard among all but Tether, Circle, and Hyperliquid. The figure follows Digital Asset's $355M a16z-led raise and reflects institutional settlement throughput from fixed participants rather than retail DeFi activity, making the cross-network comparison structural rather than directly equivalent.

title: Binance tells EU users it will no longer provide services after failing to secure MiCA license
blurb: Binance notified users across France, Italy, Poland, and Spain that it will restrict services and halt new registrations, having failed to secure a MiCA license before the July 1 deadline that requires authorization from at least one EU member state. The exchange withdrew its Greek application and plans to seek authorization in France, saying user assets remain accessible during the wind-down.

title: MoneyGram CEO on rolling out MGUSD to its 60 million users, globally
blurb: MoneyGram launched MGUSD, a proprietary US dollar stablecoin, and is rebuilding its remittance backend on blockchain rails serving 60 million users across roughly 200 countries and 500,000 retail locations. CEO Anthony Soohoo framed stablecoins as a settlement base layer, citing 24/7 weekend settlement, faster turnover of the billions in float across its network, and falling costs as liquidity deepens.

title: Securitize Goes Public on NYSE July 2 With $400M From SPAC Merger
blurb: Securitize, the tokenization platform behind BlackRock's BUIDL fund, will begin trading on the NYSE under ticker SECZ on July 2 after a Cantor Equity Partners II SPAC merger that closed with over $400M in cash. The deal included an oversubscribed $225M PIPE, the largest for an operating business entering via SPAC since 2021, with redemptions held below 30%.

title: Polymarket Confirms $3 Million Loss From Third-Party Front-End Supply-Chain Breach
blurb: Polymarket confirmed that attackers drained roughly $3M in pUSD from fewer than 15 user accounts by compromising a third-party frontend dependency, which injected a malicious script that rerouted wallet transaction approvals. Core smart contracts and backend servers were untouched, and the platform said it has removed the affected dependency and will refund impacted users in full.

title: Kraken's xStocks Opens Bending Spoons IPO Registration to EEA Retail
blurb: Kraken's xStocks platform opened registration for non-binding interest in the Bending Spoons IPO, its second pre-IPO tokenized equity offering and first for a non-US company filing on Nasdaq. Allocated EEA and select global customers will receive BSPx, a token backed 1:1 by Bending Spoons shares and tradeable 24/5 on Kraken, xStocks Alliance venues, and compatible DeFi protocols from listing day.

title: Zuckerberg Urges Meta to Explore Polymarket and Kalshi Partnerships
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg has urged Meta's leadership to explore partnerships with Polymarket and Kalshi, days after reports that the company was building a competing prediction-market app codenamed Arena. The dual-track approach would give either incumbent access to Meta's 3.56 billion daily active users, as combined event-contract volume crossed $60B in 2026 and Kalshi targets a $40B valuation after raising $1B in May.

title: U.S. House Democrat, who may soon run key committee, condemns crypto in 401(k)s
blurb: Maxine Waters, the ranking Democrat on House Financial Services, filed an 11-page comment urging the Department of Labor to withdraw its proposed rule opening 401(k) plans to crypto and other alternative assets. With Kalshi pricing an 82% chance Democrats retake the House, Waters could chair the committee next year, positioning her to pressure the SEC against the Trump-ordered expansion.

title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
blurb: A supply-chain compromise of a third-party vendor injected a malicious script into Polymarket's frontend, draining roughly $3M in pUSD from at least 11 users via phishing before the dependency was removed. PeckShield traced the funds bridged from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped into about 1,893 ETH, and Polymarket has pledged to fully refund affected users.

title: Ripple Launches RLUSD in Japan via SBI as Circle and Nomura Join Stablecoin Race
blurb: Ripple's RLUSD stablecoin went live in Japan through SBI VC Trade after the FSA classified it as a foreign-issued electronic payment instrument under the revised Payment Services Act, making it among the first foreign stablecoins approved there. The launch lands as Circle and Nomura target a 2027 USDC settlement service and Japan's three megabanks plan jointly issued yen stablecoins this fiscal year.

title: Former Ethereum Foundation leader warns of funding gap as governance shifts
blurb: Former Ethereum Foundation member Trent Van Epps warned that core protocol development needs roughly $30M annually as the Foundation reduces its central role and its treasury declines. He framed Ethereum's challenge as finding new institutions to fund public goods rather than an existential threat, noting his Protocol Guild has distributed nearly $40M to core developers over four years.

title: Base delays Beryl hard fork over B20 registry timing issue
blurb: Base postponed its Beryl hard fork to June 26 to let the B20 Activation Registry reach full operational status before the upgrade goes live. The delay ensures the registry that gates Beryl's activation is ready, avoiding a timing mismatch that could disrupt the mainnet fork's rollout.

title: 17 Democratic Senators Seek to Bar CFTC From Funding Prediction-Market State Lawsuits
blurb: Seventeen Democratic senators asked Senate appropriators to bar the CFTC from using FY2027 funds to litigate against states regulating prediction-market platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket under local gambling laws. The proposed rider would freeze the agency's nine-state campaign and any future suits by denying funding, sidestepping the underlying question of whether event contracts qualify as swaps under the Commodity Exchange Act.

title: Ripple CEO says Michael Saylor has hurt crypto market as Strategy’s STRC trades 25% below par
blurb: Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse told CNBC that Michael Saylor's financial engineering has hurt the broader crypto market, pointing to Strategy's STRC preferred shares trading 25% below their $100 par value as a "damning indictment" of the approach. The criticism landed as bitcoin fell below $60,000, MSTR slid to its lowest level since February 2024, and STRC, which carries an 11.5% annual dividend, hit a record low.

title: Tokenized Asset Value Stalls Even as Stock Token Holders Surge
blurb: Tokenized real-world asset value contracted for the first time in over a year, slipping 1.4% to roughly $31.5B over the past 30 days as price-sensitive tokenized commodities and stocks each fell about 8-9% on the week. Holders kept climbing, up 14% to 943,236, driven almost entirely by a 36% surge in tokenized stock holders to 389,972 against a falling dollar base.

title: Anti-trafficking group says Clarity Act's Section 604 could weaken accountability
blurb: The Alliance to End Human Trafficking is urging senators to revisit Section 604 of the Clarity Act, which states that developers who do not control user funds are not money transmitters, arguing the language could shield platform builders whose software facilitates trafficking-related payments. Backers counter that the provision mirrors existing FinCEN and Bank Secrecy Act guidance and leaves criminal liability under statutes like 18 U.S.C. § 1956 intact.

title: SecondFi maps recovery path after $2.4 million Cardano wallet exploit, aims to return funds within two weeks
blurb: An exploit drained roughly $2.4 million in ADA from 374 addresses over three days, exploiting a flaw in SecondFi's wallet-generation software. SecondFi has published a recovery plan and aims to return the stolen funds to affected users within two weeks.

title: BitGo Cuts Nearly 15% of Staff Six Months After IPO, Refocuses on Stablecoins and AI
blurb: BitGo is cutting nearly 15% of its workforce, around 85 to 90 employees, six months after its IPO, as CEO Mike Belshe refocuses the custodian on security, trading, stablecoins, settlement, and AI infrastructure. The $BTGO stock has fallen roughly 73% since its December listing, and the company reported a $60.7M net loss in Q1 2026.

title: Japanese financial services giant SBI Holdings to buy Bitbank for $289 million
blurb: SBI Holdings agreed to buy Japanese crypto exchange Bitbank for around $289 million, with the deal subject to regulatory approval and set to close in October. The purchase expands the Tokyo bank's crypto footprint ahead of Japan moving to classify cryptocurrencies as financial products under the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, potentially from early next fiscal year.

title: AI Bust Risks Ripple Effects From Growth to Credit, BIS Says
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements named an AI bust, alongside inflation and fiscal stress, as one of the gravest current threats to global prosperity, warning the fallout could spread from economic growth into credit markets. The flag matters for crypto because digital assets now trade with heavy correlation to AI-linked equities, leaving the sector exposed to any unwind in that trade.

title: Strategy's Enterprise mNAV Drops Below 1 for the First Time
blurb: Strategy's enterprise mNAV fell below 1 for the first time, with its combined equity, convertible debt, and preferred stock now estimated at $51.2B against a bitcoin treasury worth roughly $51.3B. The sub-1 ratio closes the equity-accretion channel central to the corporate bitcoin treasury model, since issuing shares below NAV no longer adds bitcoin-per-share, while Q1 preferred dividend obligations hit $229.5M.

title: Coinbase and OKX try to lure in Binance’s EU users after it failed to secure a MiCA license
blurb: Coinbase and OKX are offering sign-up bonuses of up to 8% to poach Binance's EU users after Binance said it would suspend some services on July 1 for lacking a MiCA license. Coinbase, MiCA-licensed since 2025, is pitching a 5% transfer bonus across Germany, France, Italy and the UK, while OKX is matching deposits up to 8% as Binance reworks its application after withdrawing its Greek filing.

title: Strategy loses its bitcoin premium as enterprise mNAV dips below 1
blurb: Strategy's enterprise mNAV slipped below 1, meaning the company's market value now trails the bitcoin on its balance sheet and erasing the premium that funded its acquisition-by-issuance model. Its STRC preferred shares briefly hit a record low of $71.40 on Friday, roughly 25% below par, signaling that investors are repricing the leveraged-treasury structure as capital-raising costs climb.

title: Chainlink Launches Project Pangea With 50+ Banks Across 16 Countries for T+0 FX Settlement
blurb: Chainlink launched Project Pangea with more than 50 banks across 16 countries, targeting T+0 atomic settlement for the $9.6T-a-day global FX market through direct Payment-versus-Payment swaps of regulated EUR and KRW stablecoins. The stack pairs Chainlink's CCIP and oracle layer with Swift's ISO 20022 messaging, letting banks route swaps through existing rails without rebuilding internal systems.

title: Tether putting $23 billion gold stockpile to work with bullion-backed loans
blurb: Tether is partnering with crypto lender Ledn to offer loans backed by its tokenized gold product XAUT, letting holders borrow against bullion rather than sell it, with both companies expected to launch the service later this year. The move monetizes Tether's roughly $23B physical gold reserve and extends to bullion the collateralized-lending model Ledn has run for bitcoin, with client collateral held 1:1 rather than lent out or used for yield.

title: Fintech Engineering Handbook
blurb: The author lays out a Fintech Engineering Handbook organizing money-handling software around three principles: no invented data, no lost data, no trust. It maps each to concrete patterns, including idempotency, deduplication, and reconciliation for integrity, event sourcing and audit trails for durability, and webhook verification and cross-source checks for distrust. The opening section on money representation argues precision decisions, from minor-units to arbitrary-precision types, propagate errors through every layer above.

title: StablecoinX Begins Nasdaq Trading as First Public ENA Treasury Vehicle
blurb: StablecoinX began trading on the Nasdaq Capital Market under ticker USDE after closing its merger with SPAC TLGY Acquisition Corp., becoming the first publicly listed ENA treasury company holding roughly 3.03 billion Ethena governance tokens worth about $275 million, or 20% of total supply. The listing gives institutions regulated equity exposure to the Ethena ecosystem without direct token custody, following recent moves by Janus Henderson, Coinbase Ventures, and Anchorage Digital into Ethena products.

title: Strategy's valuation has fallen below the value of its bitcoin holdings
blurb: Strategy's enterprise mNAV has fallen below 1, meaning the market now values the company at roughly $50.4B against bitcoin holdings worth about $51.1B, after MSTR dropped to near $82, about 85% below its November 2024 peak. Issuing new shares at these levels is dilutive to common stockholders, eroding the capital-raising flexibility that funded Saylor's bitcoin accumulation and pushing Strategy toward trading like a discounted closed-end fund.

title: MoneyGram CEO on rolling out MGUSD to its 60 million users, globally
blurb: MoneyGram launched MGUSD, a proprietary US dollar stablecoin, and is rebuilding its remittance backend on stablecoin and blockchain rails across 200 countries and 500,000 retail locations. CEO Anthony Soohoo frames the shift around settlement: stablecoins clear 24/7 including weekends, letting MoneyGram provision liquidity against live demand rather than pre-funding corridors and freeing up billions in floating cash held across its network.

title: Binance tells EU users it will no longer provide services after failing to secure MiCA license
blurb: Binance told users in France, Italy, Poland, and Spain that it will suspend services because it will not hold a MiCA license by the July 1 deadline, having withdrawn its application in Greece and halted new EU registrations. The retreat shows MiCA's single-market passport now gates EU access for even the largest exchange, with Binance pinning its re-entry on securing authorization in France.

title: Securitize Goes Public on NYSE July 2 With $400M From SPAC Merger
blurb: Securitize, the tokenization platform that issues BlackRock's BUIDL fund, will begin trading on the NYSE on July 2 under ticker SECZ after a Cantor Fitzgerald SPAC merger that closed with over $400M in cash. The deal included an oversubscribed $225M PIPE and sub-30% redemptions, giving the FINRA-approved broker-dealer fresh capital to expand its tokenized-securities custody and secondary-trading rails.

title: Kraken's xStocks Opens Bending Spoons IPO Registration to EEA Retail
blurb: Kraken's xStocks platform opened registration for eligible EEA and select global retail customers to submit non-binding interest in the Bending Spoons IPO, delivering allocations as BSPx, a 1:1 backed tokenized equity that trades on Kraken from listing day. The launch is xStocks' first tokenized offering for a non-US Nasdaq filer and follows its SpaceX debut, which drew criticism after partner platforms canceled allocations over undelivered shares.

title: Canton Network Tops Blockchain Fee Rankings With $60M in 30 Days
blurb: Canton Network, Digital Asset's permissioned blockchain for institutional settlement and tokenization, generated $60.2M in fees over the trailing 30 days, ahead of Tron's $27.6M and more than five times Ethereum's $11.3M, per DefiLlama. The figure follows Digital Asset's $355M round led by a16z crypto with HSBC, Apollo, and BNP Paribas participating, plus a Visa stablecoin-settlement pilot and Mastercard card-settlement integration.

title: Ripple Launches RLUSD in Japan via SBI as Circle and Nomura Join Stablecoin Race
blurb: Ripple's RLUSD stablecoin went live in Japan via SBI VC Trade after the JFSA classified it as a foreign-issued electronic payment instrument under the revised Payment Services Act, joining USDC and the new yen-pegged JPYSC on SBI's platform. The move makes SBI a multi-issuer access layer just as Circle and Nomura target a 2027 USDC corporate-settlement launch and Japan's three megabanks plan jointly issued yen stablecoins by fiscal year-end.

title: Ripple CEO says Michael Saylor has hurt crypto market as Strategy’s STRC trades 25% below par
blurb: Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse said on CNBC that Strategy and Michael Saylor's debt-funded bitcoin buying "has hurt the overall market," pointing to its STRC preferred shares trading 25% below their $100 par as a "damning indictment" of the model. The criticism lands as MSTR fell to its lowest since February 2024 and Strategy faces 11.5% annual dividend obligations on STRC while bitcoin trades near $58,000.

title: U.S. House Democrat, who may soon run key committee, condemns crypto in 401(k)s
blurb: Maxine Waters, the ranking Democrat on House Financial Services and likely committee chair if Democrats win the House in November, filed an 11-page comment urging the Department of Labor to withdraw its rule opening 401(k) plans to crypto and other alternative assets. She argued the proposal is incoherent while the SEC is still building investor protections for digital assets, signaling a partisan fight over Trump's push to bring crypto into retirement accounts.

title: Tokenized Asset Value Stalls Even as Stock Token Holders Surge
blurb: Tokenized real-world assets contracted for the first time in over a year, slipping 1.4% to roughly $31.5B over the past 30 days as price-sensitive tokenized commodities and stocks fell about 8-9%. Holder counts tell the opposite story, climbing 14% to 943,236 on a 36% surge in tokenized-stock holders, signaling retail adoption is outrunning the institutional Treasury flows that built the sector.

title: David Marcus on Lightspark reaching escape velocity in global payments
blurb: Lightspark CEO David Marcus said the payments network has integrated nearly 70 countries' real-time payment systems, every major stablecoin and blockchain, and a Visa stack integration for global card issuance four years after launch. Marcus framed the milestone as "escape velocity," arguing the firm can now add countries and capabilities quickly while running its Spark settlement rail for stablecoin and Bitcoin transfers at scale.

title: Japanese financial services giant SBI Holdings to buy Bitbank for $289 million
blurb: SBI Holdings agreed to buy Japanese crypto exchange Bitbank for around $289 million, with the deal expected to close in October pending regulatory approval. The acquisition extends SBI's crypto push ahead of Japan's plan to reclassify cryptocurrencies as financial products under the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, which could take effect early next fiscal year.

title: Tencent Trials TenPayGo Pay App as Foreign Visits to China Rise
blurb: Tencent is trialing TenPayGo, a standalone payment app designed to help foreign visitors navigate spending in China, where WeChat Pay and Alipay have long shut out travelers lacking domestic bank cards. The launch targets a known friction point in inbound tourism, letting overseas users link international cards to a localized wallet as China loosens visa rules and foreign arrivals climb.

title: AI Bust Risks Ripple Effects From Growth to Credit, BIS Says
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements named an AI bust, alongside inflation and fiscal stress, as one of the most alarming threats to global prosperity, warning the fallout could ripple from economic growth into credit markets. The framing puts banks and lenders on notice that AI-driven asset valuations and the debt financing the buildout are now systemic-risk concerns, not just equity-market exuberance.
